title: England

infobox.value: * 81.0% [White](/wiki/White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom "White people in the United Kingdom") * 9.6% [Asian](/wiki/British_Asians "British Asians") * 4.2% [Black](/wiki/Black_British_people "Black British people") * 3.0% [Mixed](/wiki/Mixed_(United_Kingdom_ethnicity_category) "Mixed (United Kingdom ethnicity category)") * 2.2% [other](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_the_United_Kingdom "Ethnic groups in the United Kingdom")

* 46.3% [Christianity](/wiki/Religion_in_England#Christianity "Religion in England") * 36.7% [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_the_United_Kingdom "Irreligion in the United Kingdom") * 6.7% [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_England "Islam in England") * 1.8% [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_England "Hinduism in England") * 0.9% [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism_in_England "Sikhism in England") * 0.5% [Judaism](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_England "History of the Jews in England") * 0.5% [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_England "Buddhism in England") * 0.6% [other](/wiki/Religion_in_England "Religion in England") * 6.0% not stated

[Direct rule](/wiki/Direct_rule "Direct rule") by the [UK Government](/wiki/UK_Government "UK Government") within a [parliamentary constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Westminster_parliamentary_system "Westminster parliamentary system")

infobox.key: • [House of Commons](/wiki/List_of_MPs_elected_in_the_2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of MPs elected in the 2019 United Kingdom general election")
infobox.value: [543 MPs](/wiki/List_of_MPs_for_constituencies_in_England_(2024%E2%80%93present) "List of MPs for constituencies in England (2024–present)") (of 650)
[None officially](/wiki/National_anthem_of_England "National anthem of England"); Predominantly "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")"[[a]](#cite_note-1) | |

is a [country](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") that is part of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). It is located on the island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"), of which it covers about 62%, and [more than 100 smaller adjacent islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_England "List of islands of England"). England shares [a land border](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_border "Anglo-Scottish border") with [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") to the north and [another land border](/wiki/England%E2%80%93Wales_border "England–Wales border") with [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") to the west, and is surrounded by the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the east, the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") to the south, the [Celtic Sea](/wiki/Celtic_Sea "Celtic Sea") to the south-west, and the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") to the west. [Continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe") lies to the south-east, and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") to the west. At the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census"), the population was 56,490,048. [London](/wiki/London "London") is both [the largest city](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of urban areas in the United Kingdom") and the [capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city").

**England** is a [country](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") that is part of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). It is located on the island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"), of which it covers about 62%, and [more than 100 smaller adjacent islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_England "List of islands of England"). England shares [a land border](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_border "Anglo-Scottish border") with [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") to the north and [another land border](/wiki/England%E2%80%93Wales_border "England–Wales border") with [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") to the west, and is surrounded by the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the east, the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") to the south, the [Celtic Sea](/wiki/Celtic_Sea "Celtic Sea") to the south-west, and the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") to the west. [Continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe") lies to the south-east, and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") to the west. At the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census"), the population was 56,490,048. [London](/wiki/London "London") is both [the largest city](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of urban areas in the United Kingdom") and the [capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city").

The area now called England was first inhabited by modern humans during the [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic"). It takes its name from the [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)"), a [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") tribe who settled during the 5th and 6th centuries. England became a unified state in the 10th century and has had extensive [cultural and legal impact](/wiki/Anglicisation "Anglicisation") on the wider world since the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"), which began during the 15th century.[[7]](#cite_note-11) The [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), which included Wales after 1535, ceased to be a separate [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") on 1 May 1707, when the [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") brought into effect a political union with the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") that created the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain").[[8]](#cite_note-12)

England is the origin of the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language"), the [English legal system](/wiki/English_law "English law") (which served as the basis for the [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") systems of many other countries), [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), and the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") branch of Christianity; [its parliamentary system of government](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") has been widely adopted by other nations.[[9]](#cite_note-13) The [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") began in 18th-century England, transforming its society into the world's first industrialised nation.[[10]](#cite_note-14) England is home to the two [oldest universities](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation") in the English-speaking world: the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"), founded in 1096, and the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"), founded in 1209. Both universities are ranked amongst the most prestigious in the world.[[11]](#cite_note-15)[[12]](#cite_note-16)

England's terrain chiefly consists of low hills and [plains](/wiki/Plain "Plain"), especially in the [centre](/wiki/Midlands "Midlands") and [south](/wiki/Southern_England "Southern England"). Upland and mountainous terrain is mostly found in the [north](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England") and west, including [Dartmoor](/wiki/Dartmoor "Dartmoor"), the [Lake District](/wiki/Lake_District "Lake District"), the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines"), and the [Shropshire Hills](/wiki/Shropshire_Hills "Shropshire Hills"). The [London metropolitan area](/wiki/London_metropolitan_area "London metropolitan area") has a population of over 15 million as of 2025, representing the United Kingdom's largest metropolitan area. England's population of 56.3 million comprises 84% of the population of the United Kingdom, largely concentrated around London, the [South East](/wiki/South_East_England "South East England"), and conurbations in the [Midlands](/wiki/Midlands "Midlands"), the [North West](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England"), the [North East](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England"), and [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire"), which each developed as major industrial regions during the 19th century.[[13]](#cite_note-2011_census-17)

## Toponymy

See also: [Toponymy of England](/wiki/Toponymy_of_England "Toponymy of England")

The name "England" is derived from the [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") name *Englaland*, which means "land of the [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)")".[[14]](#cite_note-18) The Angles were one of the [Germanic tribes](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") that settled in Great Britain during the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"). They came from the [Angeln](/wiki/Angeln "Angeln") region of what is now the German state of [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein").[[15]](#cite_note-19) The earliest recorded use of the term, as "*Engla londe*", is in the late-ninth-century translation into Old English of [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede")'s *[Ecclesiastical History of the English People](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_History_of_the_English_People "Ecclesiastical History of the English People")*. The term was then used to mean "the land inhabited by the English", and it included English people in what is now south-east Scotland but was then part of the English kingdom of [Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria"). The *[Anglo-Saxon Chronicle](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle")* recorded that the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") of 1086 covered the whole of England, meaning the English kingdom, but a few years later the *Chronicle* stated that King [Malcolm III](/wiki/Malcolm_III "Malcolm III") went "out of Scotlande into [Lothian](/wiki/Lothian "Lothian") in Englaland", thus using it in the more ancient sense.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMolyneaux20156–7-20)

The earliest attested reference to the Angles occurs in the 1st-century work by [Tacitus](/wiki/Tacitus "Tacitus"), *[Germania](/wiki/Germania_(book) "Germania (book)")*, in which the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word *Anglii* is used.[[17]](#cite_note-Fordham-21) The etymology of the tribal name itself is disputed by scholars; it has been suggested that it derives from the shape of the Angeln peninsula, an *angular* shape.[[18]](#cite_note-22) How and why a term derived from the name of this tribe, rather than others such as the [Saxons](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons"), came to be used for the entire country is not known, but it seems this is related to the custom of calling the Germanic people in Britain *Angli Saxones* or English Saxons to distinguish them from continental Saxons (Eald-Seaxe) of Old Saxony in Germany.[[19]](#cite_note-23) In [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic"), the Saxon tribe gave their name to the word for England (*Sasunn*);[[20]](#cite_note-24) similarly, the [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") name for the English language is *Saesneg*. A romantic name for England is [Loegria](/wiki/Lloegyr "Lloegyr"), related to the Welsh word for England, *Lloegr*, and made popular by its use in [Arthurian legend](/wiki/Arthurian_legend "Arthurian legend"). *[Albion](/wiki/Albion "Albion")* is also applied to England in a more poetic capacity,[[21]](#cite_note-25) though its original meaning is the island of Britain as a whole.

## History

Main article: [History of England](/wiki/History_of_England "History of England")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of English history](/wiki/Timeline_of_English_history "Timeline of English history").

### Prehistory

Main article: [Prehistoric Britain](/wiki/Prehistoric_Britain "Prehistoric Britain")

[![Sun shining through row of upright standing stones with other stones horizontally on the top.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Stonehenge%2C_Condado_de_Wiltshire%2C_Inglaterra%2C_2014-08-12%2C_DD_18.JPG/250px-Stonehenge%2C_Condado_de_Wiltshire%2C_Inglaterra%2C_2014-08-12%2C_DD_18.JPG)](/wiki/File:Stonehenge,_Condado_de_Wiltshire,_Inglaterra,_2014-08-12,_DD_18.JPG)

[Stonehenge](/wiki/Stonehenge "Stonehenge"), a [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") monument

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/London_-_British_Museum_-_2453.jpg/250px-London_-_British_Museum_-_2453.jpg)](/wiki/File:London_-_British_Museum_-_2453.jpg)

The [Battersea Shield](/wiki/Battersea_Shield "Battersea Shield") is one of the most significant pieces of ancient [Celtic art](/wiki/Celtic_art "Celtic art") found in Britain.

The earliest known evidence of human presence in the area now known as England was that of *[Homo antecessor](/wiki/Homo_antecessor "Homo antecessor")*, dating to about 780,000 years ago. The oldest proto-human bones discovered in England date from 500,000 years ago.[[22]](#cite_note-26) Modern humans are known to have inhabited the area during the [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic") period, though permanent settlements were only established within the last 6,000 years.[[23]](#cite_note-27) After the [last ice age](/wiki/Last_glacial_period "Last glacial period") only large mammals such as [mammoths](/wiki/Mammoth "Mammoth"), [bison](/wiki/Bison "Bison") and [woolly rhinoceros](/wiki/Woolly_rhinoceros "Woolly rhinoceros") remained. Roughly 11,000 years ago, when the [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheets "Ice sheets") began to recede, humans repopulated the area; genetic research suggests they came from the northern part of the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOppenheimer2006173-28) The sea level was lower than the present day and Britain was connected by [land bridge](/wiki/Land_bridge "Land bridge") to Ireland and [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia").[[25]](#cite_note-29)
As the seas rose, it was separated from Ireland 10,000 years ago and from Eurasia two millennia later. [Neolithic farmers](/wiki/Early_European_Farmers "Early European Farmers") from the Iberian Peninsula [migrated](/wiki/Pre-modern_human_migration "Pre-modern human migration") to the region around 4100 BC.[[26]](#cite_note-30)

The [Beaker culture](/wiki/Beaker_culture "Beaker culture") arrived around 2,500 BC, introducing drinking and food vessels constructed from clay, as well as vessels used as reduction pots to smelt copper ores.[[27]](#cite_note-31) It was during this time that major [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") monuments such as [Stonehenge](/wiki/Stonehenge "Stonehenge") (phase III) and [Avebury](/wiki/Avebury "Avebury") were constructed. By heating together tin and copper, which were in abundance in the area, the Beaker culture people made [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze"), and later iron from iron ores. The development of iron [smelting](/wiki/Smelting "Smelting") allowed the construction of better [ploughs](/wiki/Plough "Plough"), advancing agriculture (for instance, with [Celtic fields](/wiki/Celtic_field "Celtic field")), as well as the production of more effective weapons.[[28]](#cite_note-32)

During the [Iron Age](/wiki/British_Iron_Age "British Iron Age"), [Celtic culture](/wiki/Celts "Celts"), deriving from the [Hallstatt](/wiki/Hallstatt_culture "Hallstatt culture") and [La Tène cultures](/wiki/La_T%C3%A8ne_culture "La Tène culture"), arrived from Central Europe. [Brythonic](/wiki/British_language_(Celtic) "British language (Celtic)") was the spoken language during this time. Society was tribal; according to [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy")'s *[Geographia](/wiki/Geographia "Geographia")* there were around 20 tribes in the area. Like other regions on the edge of the Empire, Britain had long enjoyed trading links with the Romans. Julius Caesar of the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") attempted to [invade twice](/wiki/Caesar%27s_invasions_of_Britain "Caesar's invasions of Britain") in 55 BC; although largely unsuccessful, he managed to set up a [client king](/wiki/Roman_client_kingdoms_in_Britain "Roman client kingdoms in Britain") from the [Trinovantes](/wiki/Trinovantes "Trinovantes").

### Ancient history

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Housesteads_Roman_Fort_aerial.jpg/250px-Housesteads_Roman_Fort_aerial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Housesteads_Roman_Fort_aerial.jpg)

[Housesteads Roman fort](/wiki/Housesteads_Roman_Fort "Housesteads Roman Fort"), Northumberland, the northern limit of Roman England

The Romans invaded Britain in 43 AD during the reign of Emperor [Claudius](/wiki/Claudius "Claudius"), subsequently [conquering much of Britain](/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_Britain "Roman conquest of Britain"), and the area was incorporated into the Roman Empire as [Britannia province](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain").[[29]](#cite_note-33) The best-known of the native tribes who attempted to resist were the [Catuvellauni](/wiki/Catuvellauni "Catuvellauni") led by [Caratacus](/wiki/Caratacus "Caratacus"). Later, an uprising led by [Boudica](/wiki/Boudica "Boudica"), Queen of the [Iceni](/wiki/Iceni "Iceni"), ended with Boudica's suicide following her defeat at the [Battle of Watling Street](/wiki/Defeat_of_Boudica "Defeat of Boudica").[[30]](#cite_note-34) The author of one study of Roman Britain suggested that from 43 AD to 84 AD, the Roman invaders killed somewhere between 100,000 and 250,000 people from a population of perhaps 2,000,000.[[31]](#cite_note-35) This era saw a [Greco-Roman](/wiki/Greco-Roman "Greco-Roman") culture prevail with the introduction of [Roman law](/wiki/Roman_law "Roman law"), [Roman architecture](/wiki/Roman_architecture "Roman architecture"), [aqueducts](/wiki/List_of_aqueducts_in_the_Roman_Empire "List of aqueducts in the Roman Empire"), [sewers](/wiki/Sanitation_in_ancient_Rome "Sanitation in ancient Rome"), many agricultural items and silk.[[32]](#cite_note-36) In the 3rd century, Emperor [Septimius Severus](/wiki/Septimius_Severus "Septimius Severus") died at [Eboracum](/wiki/Eboracum "Eboracum") (now [York](/wiki/York "York")), where [Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") was subsequently proclaimed emperor a century later.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERankov199416-37)

There is debate about when Christianity was first introduced; it was no later than the 4th century, probably much earlier. According to [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede"), missionaries were sent from Rome by [Eleutherius](/wiki/Pope_Eleuterus "Pope Eleuterus") at the request of the chieftain [Lucius of Britain](/wiki/Lucius_of_Britain "Lucius of Britain") in 180 AD, to settle differences as to Eastern and Western ceremonials, which were disturbing the church. There are traditions linked to Glastonbury claiming an introduction through [Joseph of Arimathea](/wiki/Joseph_of_Arimathea "Joseph of Arimathea"), while others claim through Lucius of Britain.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWright2008143-38) By 410, during the [decline of the Roman Empire](/wiki/Decline_of_the_Roman_Empire "Decline of the Roman Empire"), Britain was left exposed by the [end of Roman rule in Britain](/wiki/End_of_Roman_rule_in_Britain "End of Roman rule in Britain") and the withdrawal of Roman army units, to defend the frontiers in continental Europe and partake in civil wars.[[35]](#cite_note-james_anglosaxons-39) Celtic Christian monastic and missionary movements flourished. This period of Christianity was influenced by ancient Celtic culture in its sensibilities, polity, practices and theology. Local "congregations" were centred in the monastic community and monastic leaders were more like chieftains, as peers, rather than in the more hierarchical system of the Roman-dominated church.[[36]](#cite_note-Lehane-40)

### Middle Ages

Main article: [England in the Middle Ages](/wiki/England_in_the_Middle_Ages "England in the Middle Ages")

[![Studded and decorated metallic mask of human face.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Sutton_Hoo_replica_%28face%29.jpg/250px-Sutton_Hoo_replica_%28face%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sutton_Hoo_replica_(face).jpg)

Replica of the 7th-century ceremonial [Sutton Hoo helmet](/wiki/Sutton_Hoo_helmet "Sutton Hoo helmet") from the [Kingdom of East Anglia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_East_Anglia "Kingdom of East Anglia")

[Roman military](/wiki/Military_of_ancient_Rome "Military of ancient Rome") withdrawals left Britain open to invasion by pagan, seafaring warriors from north-western continental Europe, chiefly the Saxons, [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)"), [Jutes](/wiki/Jutes "Jutes") and Frisians who had long raided the coasts of the Roman province. These groups then began to settle in increasing numbers over the course of the fifth and sixth centuries, initially in the eastern part of the country.[[35]](#cite_note-james_anglosaxons-39) Their advance was contained for some decades after the Britons' victory at the [Battle of Mount Badon](/wiki/Battle_of_Badon "Battle of Badon"), but subsequently resumed, overrunning the fertile lowlands of Britain and reducing the area under [Brittonic](/wiki/Britons_(historical) "Britons (historical)") control to a series of separate enclaves in the more rugged country to the west by the end of the 6th century. Contemporary texts describing this period are extremely scarce, giving rise to its description as a [Dark Age](/wiki/Dark_Age "Dark Age"). Details of the [Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain") are consequently subject to considerable disagreement; the emerging consensus is that it occurred on a large scale in the south and east but was less substantial to the north and west, where Celtic languages continued to be spoken even in areas under Anglo-Saxon control.[[37]](#cite_note-41)[[38]](#cite_note-Härke,_Heinrich_2011-42) Roman-dominated Christianity had, in general, been replaced in the conquered territories by [Anglo-Saxon paganism](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_paganism "Anglo-Saxon paganism"), but was [reintroduced by missionaries](/wiki/Christianisation_of_Anglo-Saxon_England "Christianisation of Anglo-Saxon England") from Rome led by [Augustine](/wiki/Augustine_of_Canterbury "Augustine of Canterbury") from 597.[[39]](#cite_note-43) Disputes between the Roman- and Celtic-dominated forms of Christianity ended in victory for the Roman tradition at the [Council of Whitby](/wiki/Council_of_Whitby "Council of Whitby") (664), which was ostensibly about [tonsures](/wiki/Tonsure "Tonsure") (clerical haircuts) and the date of Easter, but more significantly, about the differences in Roman and Celtic forms of authority, theology, and practice.[[36]](#cite_note-Lehane-40)

During the settlement period the lands ruled by the incomers seem to have been fragmented into numerous tribal territories, but by the 7th century, when substantial evidence of the situation again becomes available, these had coalesced into roughly a dozen kingdoms including [Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria"), [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia"), [Wessex](/wiki/Wessex "Wessex"), [East Anglia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_East_Anglia "Kingdom of East Anglia"), [Essex](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Essex "Kingdom of Essex"), [Kent](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kent "Kingdom of Kent") and [Sussex](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sussex "Kingdom of Sussex"). Over the following centuries, this process of political consolidation continued.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKirby20004-44) The 7th century saw a struggle for hegemony between Northumbria and Mercia, which in the 8th century gave way to Mercian preeminence.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELyon196023-45) In the early 9th century Mercia was displaced as the foremost kingdom by Wessex. Later in that century escalating attacks by the [Danes](/wiki/Danes_(Germanic_tribe) "Danes (Germanic tribe)") culminated in the conquest of the north and east of England, overthrowing the kingdoms of Northumbria, Mercia and East Anglia. Wessex under [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great") was left as the only surviving English kingdom, and under his successors, it steadily expanded at the expense of the kingdoms of the [Danelaw](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw"). This brought about the political unification of England, first accomplished under [Æthelstan](/wiki/%C3%86thelstan "Æthelstan") in 927 and definitively established after further conflicts by [Eadred](/wiki/Eadred "Eadred") in 953. A fresh wave of Scandinavian attacks from the late 10th century ended with the conquest of this united kingdom by [Sweyn Forkbeard](/wiki/Sweyn_Forkbeard "Sweyn Forkbeard") in 1013 and again by his son [Cnut](/wiki/Cnut_the_Great "Cnut the Great") in 1016, turning it into the centre of a short-lived [North Sea Empire](/wiki/North_Sea_Empire "North Sea Empire") that also included [Denmark](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Denmark "Kingdom of Denmark") and [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"). However, the native royal dynasty was restored with the accession of [Edward the Confessor](/wiki/Edward_the_Confessor "Edward the Confessor") in 1042.

[![King Henry V at the Battle of Agincourt, 1415.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/King_Henry_V_at_the_Battle_of_Agincourt.jpg/250px-King_Henry_V_at_the_Battle_of_Agincourt.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Henry_V_at_the_Battle_of_Agincourt.jpg)

[King Henry V](/wiki/King_Henry_V_of_England "King Henry V of England") at the [Battle of Agincourt](/wiki/Battle_of_Agincourt "Battle of Agincourt"), fought on [Saint Crispin's Day](/wiki/Saint_Crispin%27s_Day "Saint Crispin's Day") and concluded with an English victory against a larger French army in the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War")

A dispute over the succession to Edward led to an unsuccessful Norwegian Invasion in September 1066 close to York in the North, and the successful [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") in October 1066, accomplished by an army led by [Duke William of Normandy](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") invading at Hastings late September 1066.[[42]](#cite_note-46) The [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans") themselves originated from [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") and had settled in Normandy in the late 9th and early 10th centuries.[[43]](#cite_note-47) This conquest led to the almost total dispossession of the English elite and its replacement by a new French-speaking aristocracy, whose speech had a profound and permanent effect on the English language.[[44]](#cite_note-48)

Subsequently, the [House of Plantagenet](/wiki/House_of_Plantagenet "House of Plantagenet") from [Anjou](/wiki/Duchy_of_Anjou "Duchy of Anjou") inherited the English throne under [Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England"), adding England to the budding [Angevin Empire](/wiki/Angevin_Empire "Angevin Empire") of fiefs the family had inherited in France including [Aquitaine](/wiki/Duchy_of_Aquitaine "Duchy of Aquitaine").[[45]](#cite_note-Bartlett_p124-49) They reigned for three centuries, some noted monarchs being [Richard I](/wiki/Richard_I_of_England "Richard I of England"), [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England"), [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III "Edward III") and [Henry V](/wiki/Henry_V_of_England "Henry V of England").[[45]](#cite_note-Bartlett_p124-49) The period saw changes in trade and legislation, including the signing of [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta"), an English legal charter used to limit the sovereign's powers by law and protect the privileges of freemen. Catholic [monasticism](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism") flourished, providing philosophers, and the universities of Oxford and Cambridge were founded with royal patronage. The [Principality of Wales](/wiki/Principality_of_Wales "Principality of Wales") became a Plantagenet fief during the 13th century[[46]](#cite_note-50) and the [Lordship of Ireland](/wiki/Lordship_of_Ireland "Lordship of Ireland") was given to the English monarchy by the Pope. During the 14th century, the Plantagenets and the [House of Valois](/wiki/House_of_Valois "House of Valois") claimed to be legitimate claimants to the [House of Capet](/wiki/House_of_Capet "House of Capet") and of France; the two powers clashed in the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War").[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFowler1967208-51) The [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") epidemic [hit England](/wiki/Black_Death_in_England "Black Death in England"); starting in 1348, it eventually killed up to half of England's [inhabitants](/wiki/Medieval_demography "Medieval demography").[[48]](#cite_note-52)

Between 1453 and 1487, a civil war known as the [War of the Roses](/wiki/War_of_the_Roses "War of the Roses") waged between the two branches of the royal family, the [Yorkists](/wiki/House_of_York "House of York") and [Lancastrians](/wiki/House_of_Lancaster "House of Lancaster").[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrofton2007111-53) Eventually it led to the Yorkists losing the throne entirely to a Welsh noble family the [Tudors](/wiki/House_of_Tudor "House of Tudor"), a branch of the Lancastrians headed by [Henry Tudor](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") who invaded with Welsh and Breton mercenaries, gaining victory at the [Battle of Bosworth Field](/wiki/Battle_of_Bosworth_Field "Battle of Bosworth Field") where the Yorkist king [Richard III](/wiki/Richard_III_of_England "Richard III of England") was killed.[[50]](#cite_note-54)

### Early modern period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/After_Hans_Holbein_the_Younger_%281497-8-1543%29_-_Henry_VIII_%281491-1547%29_-_RCIN_404438_-_Royal_Collection.jpg/250px-After_Hans_Holbein_the_Younger_%281497-8-1543%29_-_Henry_VIII_%281491-1547%29_-_RCIN_404438_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)](/wiki/File:After_Hans_Holbein_the_Younger_(1497-8-1543)_-_Henry_VIII_(1491-1547)_-_RCIN_404438_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)

[King Henry VIII](/wiki/King_Henry_VIII "King Henry VIII") (1491–1547)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Elizabeth_I_in_Parliament_Robes.jpg/250px-Elizabeth_I_in_Parliament_Robes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elizabeth_I_in_Parliament_Robes.jpg)

[Queen Elizabeth I](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_I "Queen Elizabeth I") (1558–1603)

During the [Tudor period](/wiki/Tudor_period "Tudor period"), England began to develop [naval skills](/wiki/English_Navy "English Navy"), and exploration intensified in the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery").[[51]](#cite_note-55) [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") broke from communion with the Catholic Church, over issues relating to his divorce, under the [Acts of Supremacy](/wiki/Acts_of_Supremacy "Acts of Supremacy") in 1534 which proclaimed the monarch head of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). In contrast with much of European [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), the [roots of the split](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") were more political than theological.[[e]](#cite_note-57) He also legally incorporated his ancestral land Wales into the Kingdom of England with the [1535–1542 acts](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535%E2%80%931542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535–1542"). There were internal religious conflicts during the reigns of Henry's daughters, [Mary I](/wiki/Mary_I_of_England "Mary I of England") and [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I"). The former took the country back to Catholicism while the latter broke from it again, forcefully asserting the supremacy of [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"). The [Elizabethan era](/wiki/Elizabethan_era "Elizabethan era") is the epoch in the Tudor age of the reign of Queen Elizabeth I ("the Virgin Queen"). Historians often depict it as the [golden age](/wiki/Golden_age_(metaphor) "Golden age (metaphor)") in English history that represented the apogee of the English Renaissance and saw the flowering of great art, drama, poetry, music and literature.[[53]](#cite_note-58) England during this period had a centralised, well-organised, and effective government.[[54]](#cite_note-59)

Competing with [Spain](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), the first English colony in the Americas was founded in 1585 by explorer [Walter Raleigh](/wiki/Walter_Raleigh "Walter Raleigh") in [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") and named [Roanoke](/wiki/Roanoke_Colony "Roanoke Colony"). The Roanoke colony failed and is known as the lost colony after it was found abandoned on the return of the late-arriving supply ship.[[55]](#cite_note-60) With the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"), England also competed with the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Empire "Dutch Empire") and [French](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire") in the East. During the Elizabethan period, England was at war with Spain. An [armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada") sailed from Spain in 1588 as part of a wider plan to invade England and re-establish a Catholic monarchy. The plan was thwarted by bad coordination, stormy weather and successful harrying attacks by an English fleet under [Lord Howard of Effingham](/wiki/Charles_Howard,_1st_Earl_of_Nottingham "Charles Howard, 1st Earl of Nottingham"). This failure did not end the threat: Spain launched two further armadas, in [1596](/wiki/2nd_Spanish_Armada "2nd Spanish Armada") and [1597](/wiki/3rd_Spanish_Armada "3rd Spanish Armada"), but both were driven back by storms.

### Union with Scotland

Further information: [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns") and [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/JamesIEngland_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-JamesIEngland_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:JamesIEngland_(cropped).jpg)

[King of Scotland](/wiki/King_of_Scotland "King of Scotland"), [James VI](/wiki/King_James_VI_of_Scotland "King James VI of Scotland"), became [King of England](/wiki/King_of_England "King of England") as James I in 1603, forming the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns")

The political structure of the island changed in 1603, when the [King of Scots](/wiki/King_of_Scots "King of Scots"), [James VI](/wiki/James_VI_of_Scotland "James VI of Scotland"), a kingdom which had been a long-time rival to English interests, inherited the throne of England as James I, thereby creating a [personal union](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns").[[56]](#cite_note-Britons-61) He styled himself [King of Great Britain](/wiki/King_of_Great_Britain "King of Great Britain"), although this had no basis in English law.[[57]](#cite_note-62) Under the auspices of James VI and I the Authorised [King James Version](/wiki/King_James_Version "King James Version") of the Holy Bible was published in 1611. It was the standard version of the Bible read by most Protestant Christians for four hundred years until modern revisions were produced in the 20th century.

Based on conflicting political, religious and social positions, the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") was fought between the supporters of [Parliament](/wiki/Long_Parliament "Long Parliament") and those of King [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England"), known colloquially as [Roundheads](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") and [Cavaliers](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier") respectively. This was an interwoven part of the wider multifaceted [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms"), involving [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"). The Parliamentarians were victorious, [Charles I was executed](/wiki/Execution_of_Charles_I "Execution of Charles I") and the kingdom replaced by the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England"). Leader of the Parliament forces, [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") declared himself [Lord Protector](/wiki/Lord_Protector "Lord Protector") in 1653; a period of [personal rule](/wiki/The_Protectorate "The Protectorate") followed.[[58]](#cite_note-O_Cromwell-63) After Cromwell's death and the resignation of his son [Richard](/wiki/Richard_Cromwell "Richard Cromwell") as Lord Protector, [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") was invited to return as monarch in 1660, in a move called the [Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration"). With the reopening of theatres, fine arts, literature and performing arts flourished throughout the Restoration of the "Merry Monarch" Charles II.[[59]](#cite_note-64) After the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") of 1688, it was constitutionally established that King and Parliament should rule together, though Parliament would have the real power. This was established with the [Bill of Rights](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689") in 1689. Amongst the statutes set down were that the law could only be made by Parliament and could not be suspended by the King, also that the King could not impose taxes or raise an army without the prior approval of Parliament.[[60]](#cite_note-65) Also since that time, no British monarch has entered the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") when it is sitting, which is annually commemorated at the [State Opening of Parliament](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament") by the British monarch when the doors of the House of Commons are slammed in the face of the monarch's messenger, symbolising the rights of Parliament and its independence from the monarch.[[61]](#cite_note-66) With the founding of the [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society") in 1660, science was greatly encouraged.

[![Painting of seated male figure, with long black hair wearing a white cape and breeches.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Charles_II_of_England.jpeg/250px-Charles_II_of_England.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Charles_II_of_England.jpeg)

The [English Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration") restored the monarchy under King [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") and peace after the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War").

In 1666 the [Great Fire of London](/wiki/Great_Fire_of_London "Great Fire of London") gutted the city of London, but it was rebuilt shortly afterward with many significant buildings designed by Sir [Christopher Wren](/wiki/Christopher_Wren "Christopher Wren").[[62]](#cite_note-67) By the mid-to-late 17th century, two political factions had emerged – the [Tories](/wiki/Tory "Tory") and [Whigs](/wiki/Whigs_(British_political_party) "Whigs (British political party)"). Though the Tories initially supported Catholic king [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), some of them, along with the Whigs, during the [Revolution of 1688](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") invited the Dutch [Prince William of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") to defeat James and become the king. Some English people, especially in the north, were [Jacobites](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") and continued to support James and his sons. Under the [Stuart dynasty](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart") England expanded in trade, finance and prosperity. The Royal Navy developed Europe's largest merchant fleet.[[63]](#cite_note-68) After the parliaments of England and Scotland agreed,[[64]](#cite_note-Union_with_Scotland-69) the two countries joined in [political union](/wiki/Political_union "Political union"), to create the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") in 1707.[[56]](#cite_note-Britons-61) To accommodate the union, institutions such as the law and national churches of each remained separate.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGallagher200614-70)

### Late modern and contemporary periods

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/CanalettoSomersetHouseTerrace.jpg/250px-CanalettoSomersetHouseTerrace.jpg)](/wiki/File:CanalettoSomersetHouseTerrace.jpg)

The [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") during the [Georgian period](/wiki/Georgian_period "Georgian period") from the Terrace of Somerset House looking towards St. Paul's, c. 1750

Under the newly formed Kingdom of Great Britain, output from the Royal Society and other [English initiatives](/wiki/English_Enlightenment "English Enlightenment") combined with the [Scottish Enlightenment](/wiki/Scottish_Enlightenment "Scottish Enlightenment") to create innovations in science and engineering, while the enormous growth in [British overseas trade](/wiki/Triangular_trade "Triangular trade") protected by the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") paved the way for the establishment of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). Domestically it drove the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), a period of profound change in the [socioeconomic](/wiki/Socioeconomics "Socioeconomics") and cultural conditions of England, resulting in industrialised agriculture, manufacture, engineering and mining, as well as new and pioneering road, rail and water networks to facilitate their expansion and development.[[66]](#cite_note-71) The opening of Northwest England's [Bridgewater Canal](/wiki/Bridgewater_Canal "Bridgewater Canal") in 1761 ushered in the [canal age in Britain](/wiki/History_of_the_British_canal_system "History of the British canal system").[[67]](#cite_note-Briton20015-72) In 1825 the world's first permanent steam locomotive-hauled passenger railway – the [Stockton and Darlington Railway](/wiki/Stockton_and_Darlington_Railway "Stockton and Darlington Railway") – opened to the public.[[67]](#cite_note-Briton20015-72)

During the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), many workers moved from England's countryside to new and expanding urban industrial areas to work in factories, for instance at [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") and [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"),[[68]](#cite_note-73) with the latter the world's first industrial city.[[69]](#cite_note-Industrial_city-74) England maintained relative stability throughout the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), under [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") and [William Pitt the Younger](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger"). The [regency of George IV](/wiki/Regency_era "Regency era") is noted for its elegance and achievements in the fine arts and architecture.[[70]](#cite_note-75) During the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") planned to [invade from the south-east](/wiki/Napoleon%27s_planned_invasion_of_the_United_Kingdom "Napoleon's planned invasion of the United Kingdom"); however, this failed to manifest and the Napoleonic forces were defeated by the British: at sea by [Horatio Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson "Horatio Nelson"), and on land by [Arthur Wellesley](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington"). The major victory at the [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar") confirmed the naval supremacy Britain had established during the course of the eighteenth century.[[71]](#cite_note-76) The Napoleonic Wars fostered a concept of [Britishness](/wiki/Britishness "Britishness") and a united national [British people](/wiki/British_people "British people"), shared with the English, Scots and Welsh.[[72]](#cite_note-Colley1-77)

[![multi-storey square industrial buildings beyond a river](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Turner%2C_The_Battle_of_Trafalgar_%281822%29.jpg/250px-Turner%2C_The_Battle_of_Trafalgar_%281822%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turner,_The_Battle_of_Trafalgar_(1822).jpg)

The [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar") was a naval engagement between the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") and the combined fleets of France and Spain during the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars").[[73]](#cite_note-78)

London became the largest and most populous metropolitan area in the world during the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era"), and trade within the British Empire – as well as the standing of the British military and navy – was prestigious.[[74]](#cite_note-79) Technologically, this era saw many innovations that proved key to the United Kingdom's power and prosperity.[[75]](#cite_note-Atterbury_2011-80) Political agitation at home from radicals such as the [Chartists](/wiki/Chartism "Chartism") and the [suffragettes](/wiki/Suffragette "Suffragette") enabled legislative reform and [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage").[[76]](#cite_note-81)

Power shifts in east-central Europe led to World War I; hundreds of thousands of English soldiers died fighting for the United Kingdom as part of the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I").[[f]](#cite_note-83) Two decades later, in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the United Kingdom was again one of the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"). Developments in warfare technology saw many cities damaged by air-raids during [the Blitz](/wiki/The_Blitz "The Blitz"). Following the war, the British Empire experienced rapid [decolonisation](/wiki/Decolonisation "Decolonisation"), and there was a speeding-up of technological innovations; automobiles became the primary means of transport and [Frank Whittle](/wiki/Frank_Whittle "Frank Whittle")'s development of the [jet engine](/wiki/Jet_engine "Jet engine") led to wider [air travel](/wiki/Air_travel "Air travel").[[78]](#cite_note-84) Residential patterns were altered in England by private motoring, and by the creation of the [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service_(England) "National Health Service (England)") in 1948, providing [publicly funded health care](/wiki/Publicly_funded_health_care "Publicly funded health care") to all permanent residents free at the point of need. Combined, these prompted the reform of [local government in England](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England") in the mid-20th century.[[79]](#cite_note-85)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Frith_A_Private_View.jpg/250px-Frith_A_Private_View.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frith_A_Private_View.jpg)

The [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") is often cited as a [Golden Age](/wiki/Golden_Age "Golden Age"). Painting done by [William Powell Frith](/wiki/William_Powell_Frith "William Powell Frith") to show cultural divisions.

Since the 20th century, there has been significant population movement to England, mostly from other parts of the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), but also from the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), particularly the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent").[[80]](#cite_note-86) Since the 1970s there has been a large move away from manufacturing and an increasing emphasis on the [service industry](/wiki/Service_industry "Service industry").[[81]](#cite_note-Thatcher-87) As part of the United Kingdom, the area joined a [common market](/wiki/Common_market "Common market") initiative called the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community") which became the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").

Since the late 20th century the [administration of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Politics_of_the_United_Kingdom "Politics of the United Kingdom") has moved towards [devolved governance](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.[[82]](#cite_note-88) [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales") continues to exist as a jurisdiction within the United Kingdom.[[83]](#cite_note-BBC_Wales-89) Devolution has stimulated a greater emphasis on a more English-specific identity and patriotism.[[84]](#cite_note-90) There is no devolved English government, but an attempt to create a similar system on a sub-regional basis was rejected by [referendum](/wiki/2004_North_East_England_devolution_referendum "2004 North East England devolution referendum").[[85]](#cite_note-The_Times_2004-11-05-91)

## Governance

### Politics

Main article: [Politics of England](/wiki/Politics_of_England "Politics of England")

England is part of the United Kingdom,[[86]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-92) a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") with a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system").[[87]](#cite_note-93) There has not been a [government of England](/wiki/Government_of_England "Government of England") since 1707, when the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"),[[88]](#cite_note-94) putting into effect the terms of the [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union"), joined England and Scotland to form the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain").[[64]](#cite_note-Union_with_Scotland-69) Before the union England was ruled by [its monarch](/wiki/List_of_English_monarchs "List of English monarchs") and the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England").

[![Photograph of rectangular floodlight building, reflected in water. The building has multiple towers including one at each end. The tower on the right includes an illuminated clock face.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Palace_of_Westminster%2C_London_-_Feb_2007.jpg/330px-Palace_of_Westminster%2C_London_-_Feb_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palace_of_Westminster,_London_-_Feb_2007.jpg)

The [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster"), the seat of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") in which England is directly governed

England is governed directly by the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), although other [countries of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") have [devolved](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") governments.[[89]](#cite_note-Devolution-95) There has been debate about how to counterbalance this in England. Originally it was planned that various [regions of England](/wiki/Regions_of_England "Regions of England") would be devolved, but following the proposal's rejection by the [North East](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England") in a [2004 referendum](/wiki/2004_North_East_England_devolution_referendum "2004 North East England devolution referendum"), this has not been carried out.[[85]](#cite_note-The_Times_2004-11-05-91) In 2024, an England-only intergovernmental body, known as the [Mayoral Council for England](/wiki/Mayoral_Council_for_England "Mayoral Council for England"), was established to bring together ministers from the [UK Government](/wiki/UK_Government "UK Government"), the [Mayor of London](/wiki/Mayor_of_London "Mayor of London") and the leaders of [combined authorities](/wiki/Combined_authority "Combined authority").[[90]](#cite_note-96)

In the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") which is the [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house") of the British Parliament based at the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster"), there are 543 members of parliament (MPs) for constituencies in England, out of the 650 total.[[91]](#cite_note-97) England is represented by 347 MPs from the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), 116 from the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), 65 from the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)"), four for [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK") and four for the [Green Party of England and Wales](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales").[[92]](#cite_note-98)

### Law

Main article: [English law](/wiki/English_law "English law")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Royal_Courts_of_Justice_%286201094569%29.jpg/250px-Royal_Courts_of_Justice_%286201094569%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Courts_of_Justice_(6201094569).jpg)

The [Royal Courts of Justice](/wiki/Royal_Courts_of_Justice "Royal Courts of Justice")

The [English law](/wiki/English_law "English law") legal system, developed over the centuries, is the basis of [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law")[[93]](#cite_note-99) legal systems used in most [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") countries[[94]](#cite_note-100) and the United States (except [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana")). Despite now being part of the United Kingdom, the legal system of the [Courts of England and Wales](/wiki/Courts_of_England_and_Wales "Courts of England and Wales") continued, under the [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union"), as a separate legal system from [the one used in Scotland](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law"). The general essence of English law is that it is made by judges sitting in courts, applying their common sense and knowledge of [legal precedent](/wiki/Legal_precedent "Legal precedent") – *stare decisis* – to the facts before them.[[95]](#cite_note-101)

The court system is headed by the Senior Courts of England and Wales, consisting of the [Court of Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_of_England_and_Wales "Court of Appeal of England and Wales"), the [High Court of Justice](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice "High Court of Justice") for civil cases, and the [Crown Court](/wiki/Crown_Court "Crown Court") for criminal cases.[[96]](#cite_note-102) The [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") is the highest court for criminal and civil cases in [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"). It was created in 2009 after constitutional changes, taking over the [judicial functions of the House of Lords](/wiki/Judicial_functions_of_the_House_of_Lords "Judicial functions of the House of Lords").[[97]](#cite_note-103) A decision of the Supreme Court is binding on every other court in the hierarchy, which must follow its directions.[[98]](#cite_note-104)

The [Secretary of State for Justice](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Justice "Secretary of State for Justice") is the minister responsible to Parliament for the [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciaries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Judiciaries of the United Kingdom"), the court system and prisons and probation in England.[[99]](#cite_note-105) Crime increased between 1981 and 1995 but fell by 42% in the period 1995–2006.[[100]](#cite_note-106) The prison population doubled over the same period, giving it one of the [highest incarceration rates](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_incarceration_rate "List of countries by incarceration rate") in Western Europe at 147 per 100,000.[[101]](#cite_note-Crime_BBC-107) [His Majesty's Prison Service](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Prison_Service "His Majesty's Prison Service"), reporting to the [Ministry of Justice](/wiki/Ministry_of_Justice_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Justice (United Kingdom)"), manages most prisons, housing 81,309 prisoners in England and Wales as of September 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=England&action=edit).[[102]](#cite_note-108)

The [subdivisions of England](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_England "Subdivisions of England") consist of up to four levels of [subnational division](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division"), controlled through a variety of types of administrative entities created for the purposes of [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England").

Outside the London region, England's highest tier is the 48 [ceremonial counties](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England").[[103]](#cite_note-britannicagov-109) These are used primarily as a geographical frame of reference. Of these, 38 developed gradually since the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"); these were reformed to 51 in 1974 and to their current number in 1996.[[104]](#cite_note-maud-110) Each has a [Lord Lieutenant](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant "Lord Lieutenant") and [High Sheriff](/wiki/High_Sheriff "High Sheriff"); these posts are used to represent the [British monarch](/wiki/British_monarch "British monarch") locally.[[103]](#cite_note-britannicagov-109) Some counties, such as [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire"), are only divided further into civil parishes. The royal county of Berkshire and the metropolitan counties have different types of status to other ceremonial counties.[[105]](#cite_note-111)

The second tier is made up of [combined authorities](/wiki/Combined_authorities "Combined authorities") and the 27 [county-tier](/wiki/County_Council "County Council") [shire counties](/wiki/Shire_counties "Shire counties"). In 1974, all ceremonial counties were two-tier; and with the metropolitan county tier phased out, the 1996 reform separated the ceremonial county and the administrative county tier.

England is also divided into local government districts.[[106]](#cite_note-localgov-112) The district can align to a ceremonial county, or be a district tier within a [shire county](/wiki/Shire_counties "Shire counties"), be a [royal](/wiki/Royal_borough "Royal borough") or [metropolitan borough](/wiki/Metropolitan_borough "Metropolitan borough"), have [borough](/wiki/Borough_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "Borough status in the United Kingdom") or [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom"), or be a [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England").

At the community level, much of England is divided into [civil parishes](/wiki/Civil_parishes_in_England "Civil parishes in England") with their own [councils](/wiki/Parish_councils_in_England "Parish councils in England"); in Greater London only one such parish, [Queen's Park](/wiki/Queen%27s_Park,_London "Queen's Park, London"), exists as of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=England&action=edit) after they were [abolished in 1965](/wiki/London_Government_Act_1963 "London Government Act 1963") until legislation [allowed their recreation](/wiki/Local_Government_and_Public_Involvement_in_Health_Act_2007 "Local Government and Public Involvement in Health Act 2007") in 2007.

#### London

From 1994 until the early 2010s England was divided for a few purposes into regions; a [1998 referendum](/wiki/1998_Greater_London_Authority_referendum "1998 Greater London Authority referendum") for the London Region created the [London Assembly](/wiki/London_Assembly "London Assembly") two years later.[[107]](#cite_note-113) A failed [2004 North East England devolution referendum](/wiki/2004_North_East_England_devolution_referendum "2004 North East England devolution referendum") cancelled further [regional assembly](/wiki/Regional_Assemblies_in_England "Regional Assemblies in England") devolution[[85]](#cite_note-The_Times_2004-11-05-91) with the regional structure outside London abolished.

Administratively, London is divided between 33 [local government districts](/wiki/Local_government_districts "Local government districts"): the 32 [London boroughs](/wiki/London_boroughs "London boroughs") and the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London").[[108]](#cite_note-114) The 32 London boroughs form the ceremonial county of Greater London, with the City of London being a separate ceremonial county.

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of England](/wiki/Geography_of_England "Geography of England")

### Landscape and rivers

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Malvern_Hills_-_England.jpg/250px-Malvern_Hills_-_England.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malvern_Hills_-_England.jpg)

The [Malvern Hills](/wiki/Malvern_Hills "Malvern Hills") located in the English counties of [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") and [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire"). The hills have been designated by the Countryside Agency as an [Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty](/wiki/Area_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty "Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty").

Geographically, England includes the central and southern two-thirds of the island of Great Britain, plus such offshore islands as the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight") and the [Isles of Scilly](/wiki/Isles_of_Scilly "Isles of Scilly"). It is bordered by two other countries of the United Kingdom: [to the north](/wiki/Anglo_Scottish_border "Anglo Scottish border") by Scotland and [to the west](/wiki/Wales-England_border "Wales-England border") by Wales.

England is closer than any other part of mainland Britain to the European continent. It is separated from [France](/wiki/France "France") ([Hauts-de-France](/wiki/Hauts-de-France "Hauts-de-France")) by a 21-mile (34 km)[[109]](#cite_note-Engchannel-115) sea gap, though the two countries are connected by the [Channel Tunnel](/wiki/Channel_Tunnel "Channel Tunnel") near [Folkestone](/wiki/Folkestone "Folkestone").[[110]](#cite_note-116) England also has shores on the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea"), [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") and Atlantic Ocean.

The ports of London, [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), and [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne") lie on the tidal rivers [Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames"), [Mersey](/wiki/River_Mersey "River Mersey") and [Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne") respectively. At 220 miles (350 km), the [Severn](/wiki/River_Severn "River Severn") is the longest river flowing through England.[[111]](#cite_note-117) It empties into the [Bristol Channel](/wiki/Bristol_Channel "Bristol Channel") and is notable for its [Severn Bore](/wiki/Severn_Bore "Severn Bore") (a [tidal bore](/wiki/Tidal_bore "Tidal bore")), which can reach 2 metres (6.6 ft) in height.[[112]](#cite_note-118) However, the longest river entirely in England is the Thames, which is 215 miles (346 km) in length.[[113]](#cite_note-Thames-119) There are many [lakes in England](/wiki/Lakes_in_England "Lakes in England"); the largest is [Windermere](/wiki/Windermere "Windermere"), within the aptly named [Lake District](/wiki/Lake_District "Lake District").[[114]](#cite_note-metoffice_nw-120)

Most of England's landscape consists of low hills and plains, with upland and mountainous terrain in the north and west of the country. The northern uplands include the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines"), a chain of uplands dividing east and west, the Lake District mountains in Cumbria, and the [Cheviot Hills](/wiki/Cheviot_Hills "Cheviot Hills"), straddling the border between England and Scotland. The highest point in England, at 978 metres (3,209 ft), is [Scafell Pike](/wiki/Scafell_Pike "Scafell Pike") in the Lake District.[[114]](#cite_note-metoffice_nw-120) The [Shropshire Hills](/wiki/Shropshire_Hills "Shropshire Hills") are near Wales while [Dartmoor](/wiki/Dartmoor "Dartmoor") and [Exmoor](/wiki/Exmoor "Exmoor") are two upland areas in the south-west of the country. The approximate dividing line between terrain types is often indicated by the [Tees–Exe line](/wiki/Tees%E2%80%93Exe_line "Tees–Exe line").[[115]](#cite_note-121)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Glenridding%2C_Cumbria%2C_England_-_June_2009.jpg/250px-Glenridding%2C_Cumbria%2C_England_-_June_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glenridding,_Cumbria,_England_-_June_2009.jpg)

The village of [Glenridding](/wiki/Glenridding "Glenridding") and [Ullswater](/wiki/Ullswater "Ullswater") in [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria")

The Pennines, known as the "backbone of England", are the oldest range of mountains in the country, originating from the end of the [Paleozoic Era](/wiki/Paleozoic_Era "Paleozoic Era") around 300 million years ago.[[116]](#cite_note-122) Their geological composition includes, amongst others, [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") and [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), and also coal. There are [karst](/wiki/Karst "Karst") landscapes in calcite areas such as parts of [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire") and [Derbyshire](/wiki/Derbyshire "Derbyshire"). The Pennine landscape is high [moorland](/wiki/Moorland "Moorland") in upland areas, indented by fertile valleys of the region's rivers. They contain two [national parks](/wiki/National_parks_in_England "National parks in England"), the [Yorkshire Dales](/wiki/Yorkshire_Dales "Yorkshire Dales") and the [Peak District](/wiki/Peak_District "Peak District"). In the [West Country](/wiki/West_Country "West Country"), Dartmoor and Exmoor of the Southwest Peninsula include upland moorland supported by granite.[[117]](#cite_note-123)

The [English Lowlands](/wiki/English_Lowlands_beech_forests "English Lowlands beech forests") are in the central and southern regions of the country, consisting of green rolling hills, including the [Cotswold Hills](/wiki/Cotswold_Hills "Cotswold Hills"), [Chiltern Hills](/wiki/Chiltern_Hills "Chiltern Hills"), [North](/wiki/North_Downs "North Downs") and [South Downs](/wiki/South_Downs "South Downs"); where they meet the sea they form white rock exposures such as the [cliffs of Dover](/wiki/Cliffs_of_Dover "Cliffs of Dover"). This also includes relatively flat plains such as the [Salisbury Plain](/wiki/Salisbury_Plain "Salisbury Plain"), [Somerset Levels](/wiki/Somerset_Levels "Somerset Levels"), [South Coast Plain](/wiki/South_Coast_Plain "South Coast Plain") and [The Fens](/wiki/The_Fens "The Fens").

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of England](/wiki/Climate_of_England "Climate of England")

England has a [temperate](/wiki/Temperate_climate "Temperate climate") [maritime climate](/wiki/Maritime_climate "Maritime climate"): it is mild with temperatures not much lower than 0 °C (32 °F) in winter and not much higher than 32 °C (90 °F) in summer.[[118]](#cite_note-weather-124) The weather is damp relatively frequently and is changeable. The coldest months are January and February, the latter particularly on the [English coast](/wiki/Geography_of_the_United_Kingdom "Geography of the United Kingdom"), while July is normally the warmest month. Months with mild to warm weather are May, June, September and October.[[118]](#cite_note-weather-124) Rainfall is spread fairly evenly throughout the year.

Important influences on the climate of England are its proximity to the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), its northern [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") and the warming of the sea by the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream").[[118]](#cite_note-weather-124) Rainfall is higher in the west, and parts of the [Lake District](/wiki/Lake_District "Lake District") receive more rain than anywhere else in the country.[[118]](#cite_note-weather-124) Since weather records began, the highest temperature recorded was 40.3 °C (104.5 °F) on 19 July 2022 at [Coningsby](/wiki/Coningsby "Coningsby"), [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire"),[[119]](#cite_note-125) while the lowest was −26.1 °C (−15.0 °F) on 10 January 1982 in [Edgmond](/wiki/Edgmond,_Shropshire "Edgmond, Shropshire"), Shropshire.[[120]](#cite_note-126)

### Nature and wildlife

Main article: [Fauna of England](/wiki/Fauna_of_England "Fauna of England")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Eurasian_Wren_%28Troglodytes_troglodytes%29%2C_Icklesham%2C_Sussex.jpg/250px-Eurasian_Wren_%28Troglodytes_troglodytes%29%2C_Icklesham%2C_Sussex.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eurasian_Wren_(Troglodytes_troglodytes),_Icklesham,_Sussex.jpg)

The [Eurasian wren](/wiki/Eurasian_wren "Eurasian wren"), the most numerous bird species in England[[121]](#cite_note-127)

The fauna of England is similar to that of other areas in the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") with a wide range of vertebrate and invertebrate life in a diverse range of habitats.[[122]](#cite_note-128) [National nature reserves in England](/wiki/National_nature_reserves_in_England "National nature reserves in England") are designated by [Natural England](/wiki/Natural_England "Natural England") as key places for [wildlife](/wiki/Wildlife "Wildlife") and natural features in England. They were established to protect the most significant areas of habitat and of geological formations. NNRs are managed on behalf of the nation, many by Natural England themselves, but also by non-governmental organisations, including the members of [The Wildlife Trusts](/wiki/The_Wildlife_Trusts "The Wildlife Trusts") partnership, the [National Trust](/wiki/National_Trust_for_Places_of_Historic_Interest_or_Natural_Beauty "National Trust for Places of Historic Interest or Natural Beauty"), and the [Royal Society for the Protection of Birds](/wiki/Royal_Society_for_the_Protection_of_Birds "Royal Society for the Protection of Birds"). There are 221 NNRs in England covering 110,000 hectares (1,100 square kilometres). Often they contain rare species or nationally important populations of plants and animals.[[123]](#cite_note-129)
.
The [Environment Agency](/wiki/Environment_Agency "Environment Agency") is a non-departmental public body, established in 1995 and sponsored by the [Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs](/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs "Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs") with responsibilities relating to the protection and enhancement of the environment in England.[[124]](#cite_note-130) The [Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs "Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs") is the minister responsible for environmental protection, agriculture, fisheries and rural communities in England.[[125]](#cite_note-131)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Richmond_Park_-_London_-_England_-_02102005.jpg/250px-Richmond_Park_-_London_-_England_-_02102005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Richmond_Park_-_London_-_England_-_02102005.jpg)

[Red deer](/wiki/Red_deer "Red deer") in [Richmond Park](/wiki/Richmond_Park "Richmond Park"). The park was created by [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") in the 17th century as a [deer park](/wiki/Deer_park_(England) "Deer park (England)").[[126]](#cite_note-Pastscape-132)

England has a [temperate oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") in most areas, lacking extremes of cold or heat, but does have a few small areas of [subarctic](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") and warmer areas in [the South West](/wiki/South_West_England "South West England"). Towards the [North of England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England") the climate becomes colder and most of England's mountains and high hills are located here and have a major impact on the climate and thus the local fauna of the areas. [Deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") woodlands are common across all of England and provide a great habitat for much of England's wildlife, but these give way in northern and upland areas of England to [coniferous](/wiki/Coniferous "Coniferous") forests (mainly plantations) which also benefit certain forms of wildlife. Some species have adapted to the expanded urban environment, particularly the [red fox](/wiki/Red_fox "Red fox"), which is the most successful [urban mammal](/wiki/Urban_wildlife "Urban wildlife") after the [brown rat](/wiki/Brown_rat "Brown rat"), and other animals such as [common wood pigeon](/wiki/Common_wood_pigeon "Common wood pigeon"), both of which thrive in urban and suburban areas.[[127]](#cite_note-133)

### Major conurbations

See also: [List of places in England](/wiki/List_of_places_in_England "List of places in England")

The [Greater London Built-up Area](/wiki/Greater_London_Built-up_Area "Greater London Built-up Area") is by far the largest urban area in England[[128]](#cite_note-BUA-134) and one of the busiest cities in the world. It is considered a [global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city") and has a population larger than any other country in the United Kingdom besides England itself.[[128]](#cite_note-BUA-134) Other urban areas of considerable size and influence tend to be in [northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England") or the [English Midlands](/wiki/English_Midlands "English Midlands").[[128]](#cite_note-BUA-134) There are [50 settlements](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of cities in the United Kingdom") which have designated [city status in England](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom"), while the wider United Kingdom has 66.

While many cities in England are quite large, such as [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield"), [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), [Leeds](/wiki/City_of_Leeds "City of Leeds"), [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne"), [Bradford](/wiki/City_of_Bradford "City of Bradford"), [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham"), population size is not a prerequisite for city status.[[129]](#cite_note-cathcities-135) Traditionally the status was given to towns with [diocesan cathedrals](/wiki/List_of_cathedrals_in_England "List of cathedrals in England"), so there are smaller cities like [Wells](/wiki/Wells,_Somerset "Wells, Somerset"), [Ely](/wiki/Ely,_Cambridgeshire "Ely, Cambridgeshire"), [Ripon](/wiki/Ripon "Ripon"), [Truro](/wiki/Truro "Truro") and [Chichester](/wiki/Chichester "Chichester").
