## Economy

Main article: [Economy of England](/wiki/Economy_of_England "Economy of England")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Canary_Wharf_Wide_View_2%2C_London_-_July_2009-2.jpg/250px-Canary_Wharf_Wide_View_2%2C_London_-_July_2009-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canary_Wharf_Wide_View_2,_London_-_July_2009-2.jpg)

[London](/wiki/London "London") is the financial capital of England and the United Kingdom.

England's economy is one of the largest and most dynamic in the world, with a [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita") of £37,852 in 2022.[[6]](#cite_note-ONS_GDP-10) [HM Treasury](/wiki/HM_Treasury "HM Treasury"), led by the [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer"), is responsible for developing and executing the government's [public finance](/wiki/Public_finance "Public finance") policy and [economic policy](/wiki/Economic_policy "Economic policy").[[130]](#cite_note-136) Usually regarded as a [mixed market economy](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_economy "Anglo-Saxon economy"), it has adopted many [free market](/wiki/Free_market "Free market") principles, yet maintains an advanced [social welfare](/wiki/Social_welfare "Social welfare") infrastructure.[[131]](#cite_note-137)

The economy of England is the largest part of the [UK's economy](/wiki/Economy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Economy of the United Kingdom").[[132]](#cite_note-regionalacc-138) England is a leader in the chemical and pharmaceutical sectors and in key technical industries, particularly [aerospace](/wiki/Aerospace "Aerospace"), the [arms industry](/wiki/Arms_industry "Arms industry"), and the [software industry](/wiki/Software_industry "Software industry"). London, home to the [London Stock Exchange](/wiki/London_Stock_Exchange "London Stock Exchange"), the United Kingdom's main [stock exchange](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange") and the largest in Europe, is England's financial centre, with 100 of Europe's 500 largest corporations being based there.[[133]](#cite_note-139) As of 2025, London is the largest financial centre in Europe and the second largest in the world.[[134]](#cite_note-140)

London has been named as the fastest growing technology hub in Europe, with England having over 100 unique tech companies with a value of $1 billion or more.[[135]](#cite_note-141)[[136]](#cite_note-142) The [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"), founded in 1694 as private banker to the government of England and a [state-owned institution](/wiki/Nationalisation "Nationalisation") since 1946, is the United Kingdom's [central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank").[[137]](#cite_note-143) The bank has a monopoly on the issue of banknotes in [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"), although not in other parts of the UK. The government has devolved responsibility to the bank's [Monetary Policy Committee](/wiki/Monetary_Policy_Committee_(United_Kingdom) "Monetary Policy Committee (United Kingdom)") for managing the monetary policy of the country and setting interest rates.[[138]](#cite_note-144)

[![A grey coloured convertible.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Aston_Martin_DB12_Volante_IMG_9323.jpg/250px-Aston_Martin_DB12_Volante_IMG_9323.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aston_Martin_DB12_Volante_IMG_9323.jpg)

[Aston Martin](/wiki/Aston_Martin "Aston Martin") manufacture luxury vehicles in England.

England is highly industrialised, but since the 1970s there has been a decline in traditional heavy and manufacturing industries, and an increasing emphasis on a more [service industry](/wiki/Service_industry "Service industry")-oriented economy.[[81]](#cite_note-Thatcher-87) Tourism has become a significant industry, attracting millions of visitors to England each year. The export part of the economy is dominated by [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Pharmaceuticals "Pharmaceuticals"), automotives, [crude oil](/wiki/Crude_oil "Crude oil") and petroleum from the English parts of [North Sea oil](/wiki/North_Sea_oil "North Sea oil") along with [Wytch Farm](/wiki/Wytch_Farm "Wytch Farm"), [aircraft engines](/wiki/Aircraft_engine "Aircraft engine") and alcoholic beverages.[[139]](#cite_note-145) The [creative industries](/wiki/Creative_industries "Creative industries") accounted for 7 per cent GVA in 2005 and grew at an average of 6 per cent per annum between 1997 and 2005.[[140]](#cite_note-146)

Agriculture is intensive, highly mechanised and efficient by European standards, producing 60% of food needs with only 2% of the labour force.[[141]](#cite_note-147) Two-thirds of production is devoted to livestock, the remainder to arable crops.[[142]](#cite_note-148) The main crops that are grown are [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"), [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley"), [oats](/wiki/Oat "Oat"), [potatoes](/wiki/Potato "Potato"), and [sugar beets](/wiki/Sugar_beet "Sugar beet"). England retains a significant fishing industry. Its fleets bring home a variety of fish, ranging from [sole](/wiki/Sole_(fish) "Sole (fish)") to [herring](/wiki/Herring "Herring"). England is also rich in natural resources including [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal"), [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"), [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas"), [tin](/wiki/Tin "Tin"), [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), [iron ore](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore"), [salt](/wiki/Salt "Salt"), [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"), [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk"), [gypsum](/wiki/Gypsum "Gypsum"), [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead"), and silica.[[143]](#cite_note-149)

### Science and technology

Main articles: [List of English inventions and discoveries](/wiki/List_of_English_inventions_and_discoveries "List of English inventions and discoveries") and [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society")

Prominent English figures from the field of science and mathematics include Sir [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton"), [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin"), [Robert Hooke](/wiki/Robert_Hooke "Robert Hooke"), [Alan Turing](/wiki/Alan_Turing "Alan Turing"), [Stephen Hawking](/wiki/Stephen_Hawking "Stephen Hawking"), [Edward Jenner](/wiki/Edward_Jenner "Edward Jenner"), [Francis Crick](/wiki/Francis_Crick "Francis Crick"), [Joseph Lister](/wiki/Joseph_Lister,_1st_Baron_Lister "Joseph Lister, 1st Baron Lister"), [Joseph Priestley](/wiki/Joseph_Priestley "Joseph Priestley"), [Thomas Young](/wiki/Thomas_Young_(scientist) "Thomas Young (scientist)"), [Christopher Wren](/wiki/Christopher_Wren "Christopher Wren") and [Richard Dawkins](/wiki/Richard_Dawkins "Richard Dawkins").

[![Torso of man with long white hair and dark coloured jacket](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/GodfreyKneller-IsaacNewton-1689.jpg/250px-GodfreyKneller-IsaacNewton-1689.jpg)](/wiki/File:GodfreyKneller-IsaacNewton-1689.jpg)

Sir [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") is one of the most influential figures in the [history of science](/wiki/History_of_science "History of science").

England was a leading centre of the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution") from the 17th century.[[144]](#cite_note-150) As the birthplace of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), England was home to many significant inventors during the late 18th and early 19th centuries. Famous English engineers include [Isambard Kingdom Brunel](/wiki/Isambard_Kingdom_Brunel "Isambard Kingdom Brunel"), best known for the creation of the [Great Western Railway](/wiki/Great_Western_Railway "Great Western Railway"), a series of famous [steamships](/wiki/Steamship "Steamship"), and numerous important bridges, revolutionising public transport and modern-day engineering.[[145]](#cite_note-151) [Thomas Newcomen](/wiki/Thomas_Newcomen "Thomas Newcomen")'s [steam engine](/wiki/Newcomen_steam_engine "Newcomen steam engine") helped spawn the Industrial Revolution.[[146]](#cite_note-152)

The Father of Railways, [George Stephenson](/wiki/George_Stephenson "George Stephenson"), built the first public inter-city railway line in the world, the [Liverpool and Manchester Railway](/wiki/Liverpool_and_Manchester_Railway "Liverpool and Manchester Railway"), which opened in 1830. With his role in the marketing and manufacturing of the steam engine, and invention of modern coinage, [Matthew Boulton](/wiki/Matthew_Boulton "Matthew Boulton") (business partner of [James Watt](/wiki/James_Watt "James Watt")) is regarded as one of the most influential entrepreneurs in history.[[147]](#cite_note-153) The physician [Edward Jenner](/wiki/Edward_Jenner "Edward Jenner")'s [smallpox vaccine](/wiki/Smallpox_vaccine "Smallpox vaccine") is said to have "saved more lives ... than were lost in all the wars of mankind since the beginning of recorded history."[[148]](#cite_note-154)

Inventions and discoveries of the English include the [jet engine](/wiki/Jet_engine "Jet engine"); the first industrial [spinning machine](/wiki/Spinning_frame "Spinning frame"); [the first computer](/wiki/Analytical_engine "Analytical engine") and the first [modern computer](/wiki/Manchester_Baby "Manchester Baby"); the [World Wide Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web") along with [HTML](/wiki/HTML "HTML"); the first successful human [blood transfusion](/wiki/Blood_transfusion "Blood transfusion"); the motorised [vacuum cleaner](/wiki/Vacuum_cleaner "Vacuum cleaner");[[149]](#cite_note-americanheritage-155) the [lawn mower](/wiki/Lawn_mower "Lawn mower"); the [seat belt](/wiki/Seat_belt "Seat belt"); the [hovercraft](/wiki/Hovercraft "Hovercraft"); the [electric motor](/wiki/Electric_motor "Electric motor"); [steam engines](/wiki/Steam_engine "Steam engine"); and theories such as the Darwinian theory of [evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution") and [atomic theory](/wiki/Atomic_theory "Atomic theory"). Newton developed the ideas of [universal gravitation](/wiki/Universal_gravitation "Universal gravitation"), [Newtonian mechanics](/wiki/Newtonian_mechanics "Newtonian mechanics"), and [calculus](/wiki/Calculus "Calculus"), and [Robert Hooke](/wiki/Robert_Hooke "Robert Hooke") his eponymously named [law of elasticity](/wiki/Hooke%27s_law_of_elasticity "Hooke's law of elasticity"). Other inventions include the iron plate railway, the [thermosiphon](/wiki/Thermosiphon "Thermosiphon"), [tarmac](/wiki/Tarmacadam "Tarmacadam"), the [rubber band](/wiki/Rubber_band "Rubber band"), the [mousetrap](/wiki/Mousetrap "Mousetrap"), ["cat's eye"](/wiki/Cat%27s_eye_(road) "Cat's eye (road)") [road marker](/wiki/Raised_pavement_marker "Raised pavement marker"), joint development of the [light bulb](/wiki/Light_bulb "Light bulb"), steam [locomotives](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive"), the modern [seed drill](/wiki/Seed_drill "Seed drill") and many modern techniques and technologies used in [precision engineering](/wiki/Precision_engineering "Precision engineering").[[150]](#cite_note-invent-156)

English innovators made crucial contributions to computing from the earliest concepts to the microchip era. [Charles Babbage](/wiki/Charles_Babbage "Charles Babbage")[[151]](#cite_note-157) and [Ada Lovelace](/wiki/Ada_Lovelace "Ada Lovelace")[[152]](#cite_note-158) conceived the first programmable computer, while [George Boole](/wiki/George_Boole "George Boole") created the binary logic underlying all digital systems, known as [boolean logic](/wiki/Boolean_logic "Boolean logic").[[153]](#cite_note-159) [Alan Turing](/wiki/Alan_Turing "Alan Turing") defined the foundations of computing and pioneered artificial intelligence,[[154]](#cite_note-160) while [Tommy Flowers](/wiki/Tommy_Flowers "Tommy Flowers") built [Colossus](/wiki/Colossus_computer "Colossus computer"), proving electronic computing feasible.[[155]](#cite_note-161) [Sir Frederic Williams](/wiki/Frederic_C._Williams "Frederic C. Williams"), [Tom Kilburn](/wiki/Tom_Kilburn "Tom Kilburn") and [Geoff Tootill](/wiki/Geoff_Tootill "Geoff Tootill") developed the [Manchester Baby](/wiki/Manchester_Baby "Manchester Baby"),[[156]](#cite_note-162) while [Sir Maurice Wilkes](/wiki/Maurice_Wilkes "Maurice Wilkes") developed [EDSAC](/wiki/EDSAC "EDSAC")[[157]](#cite_note-163) contributing to the first stored-program computers that established modern computer architecture. [Geoffrey Dummer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Dummer "Geoffrey Dummer") conceptualised the integrated circuit.[[158]](#cite_note-164)

The [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society"), formally [The Royal Society of London for Improving Natural Knowledge](/wiki/The_Royal_Society_of_London_for_Improving_Natural_Knowledge "The Royal Society of London for Improving Natural Knowledge"),[[159]](#cite_note-royalsociety.org-165) is a [learned society](/wiki/Learned_society "Learned society") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")'s national [academy of sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_sciences "Academy of sciences"). Founded on 28 November 1660,[[159]](#cite_note-royalsociety.org-165) It is the oldest national scientific institution in the world.[[160]](#cite_note-166) The [Royal Institution of Great Britain](/wiki/Royal_Institution "Royal Institution") was founded in 1799 by leading English scientists, including [Henry Cavendish](/wiki/Henry_Cavendish "Henry Cavendish").[[161]](#cite_note-167) Some experts claim that the earliest concept of a [metric system](/wiki/Metric_system "Metric system") was invented by [John Wilkins](/wiki/John_Wilkins "John Wilkins") in 1668.[[162]](#cite_note-168)

Scientific research and development remains important in the [universities of England](/wiki/Universities_of_England "Universities of England"), with many establishing [science parks](/wiki/Science_park "Science park") to facilitate production and co-operation with industry.[[163]](#cite_note-169) [Cambridge](/wiki/Silicon_Fen "Silicon Fen") is the most intensive research cluster for science and technology in the world.[[164]](#cite_note-170) In 2022, the UK produced 6.3 per cent of the world's scientific research papers and had a 10.5 per cent share of scientific citations, the third highest in the world (after the United States and China).[[165]](#cite_note-171) Scientific journals produced in England include *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")*, the *[British Medical Journal](/wiki/BMJ "BMJ")* and *[The Lancet](/wiki/The_Lancet "The Lancet")*. The [Department for Science, Innovation and Technology](/wiki/Department_for_Science,_Innovation_and_Technology "Department for Science, Innovation and Technology"), [Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Science,_Innovation_and_Technology "Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology"), and [Minister of State for Science, Research and Innovation](/wiki/Minister_of_State_for_Science,_Research_and_Innovation "Minister of State for Science, Research and Innovation") has responsibility for science in England.[[166]](#cite_note-172)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in England](/wiki/Transport_in_England "Transport in England")

The [Department for Transport](/wiki/Department_for_Transport "Department for Transport") is the government body responsible for overseeing transport in England. The department is run by the [Secretary of State for Transport](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Transport "Secretary of State for Transport").

England has a dense and modern transportation infrastructure. There are [many motorways in England](/wiki/List_of_motorways_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of motorways in the United Kingdom"), and many other trunk roads, such as the [A1 Great North Road](/wiki/A1_road_(Great_Britain) "A1 road (Great Britain)"), which runs through eastern England from London to Newcastle[[167]](#cite_note-roads-173) (much of this section is motorway) and onward to the Scottish border. The longest motorway in England is the [M6](/wiki/M6_motorway "M6 motorway"), from [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby,_Warwickshire "Rugby, Warwickshire") through the [North West](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England") up to the [Anglo-Scottish border](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_border "Anglo-Scottish border"), a distance of 232 miles (373 km).[[167]](#cite_note-roads-173) Other major routes include: the [M1](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway") from London to Leeds, the [M25](/wiki/M25_motorway "M25 motorway") which encircles London, the [M60](/wiki/M60_motorway_(Great_Britain) "M60 motorway (Great Britain)") which encircles Manchester, the [M4](/wiki/M4_motorway "M4 motorway") from London to South Wales, the [M62](/wiki/M62_motorway "M62 motorway") from Liverpool via Manchester to East Yorkshire, and the [M5](/wiki/M5_motorway "M5 motorway") from Birmingham to Bristol and the South West.[[167]](#cite_note-roads-173)

[![red stone building with tall clock tower in corner](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/St_Pancras_Railway_Station_2012-06-23.jpg/250px-St_Pancras_Railway_Station_2012-06-23.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Pancras_Railway_Station_2012-06-23.jpg)

[London St Pancras International](/wiki/St_Pancras_railway_station "St Pancras railway station") is one of London's main domestic and international transport hubs providing both commuter rail and high-speed rail services across the UK and to Paris, [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille") and [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels").

Bus transport across the country is widespread; major companies include [Arriva](/wiki/Arriva_UK_Bus "Arriva UK Bus"), [FirstGroup](/wiki/FirstGroup "FirstGroup"), [Go-Ahead Group](/wiki/Go-Ahead_Group "Go-Ahead Group"), [Mobico Group](/wiki/Mobico_Group "Mobico Group"), [Rotala](/wiki/Rotala "Rotala") and [Stagecoach Group](/wiki/Stagecoach_Group "Stagecoach Group"). [Bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") originated in England with the [Runcorn](/wiki/Runcorn "Runcorn") Busway opening in 1971.[[168]](#cite_note-Runcorn_Busway_JSTOR-174)[[169]](#cite_note-RUDI_7.3_Transport-175) The red [double-decker buses](/wiki/Double-decker_bus "Double-decker bus") in London have become a symbol of England. [National Cycle Route](/wiki/National_Cycle_Route "National Cycle Route") offers cycling routes nationally.

[Rail transport in England](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Great_Britain "Rail transport in Great Britain") is the oldest in the world: passenger railways originated in England in 1825.[[170]](#cite_note-176) Much of Britain's 10,000 miles (16,000 km) of rail network lies in England, covering the country fairly extensively. There is rail transport access to France and Belgium through an undersea rail link, the [Channel Tunnel](/wiki/Channel_Tunnel "Channel Tunnel"), which was completed in 1994.

[Great British Railways](/wiki/Great_British_Railways "Great British Railways") is a planned state-owned public body that will oversee [rail transport in Great Britain](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Great_Britain "Rail transport in Great Britain") from 2024. The [Office of Rail and Road](/wiki/Office_of_Rail_and_Road "Office of Rail and Road") is responsible for the economic and safety regulation of England's railways.[[171]](#cite_note-177) [Crossrail](/wiki/Crossrail "Crossrail") was Europe's largest construction project with a £15 billion projected cost, opened in 2022.[[172]](#cite_note-178) [High Speed 2](/wiki/High_Speed_2 "High Speed 2"), a high-speed north–south railway line, is under construction since 2019.[[173]](#cite_note-179)

There is a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") network in two English cities: the [London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground"), and the [Tyne and Wear Metro](/wiki/Tyne_and_Wear_Metro "Tyne and Wear Metro") in [Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne"), [Gateshead](/wiki/Gateshead "Gateshead") and [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland "Sunderland").[[174]](#cite_note-pubwhite-180) There are several extensive tram networks, such as the [Manchester Metrolink](/wiki/Manchester_Metrolink "Manchester Metrolink"), [Sheffield Supertram](/wiki/Sheffield_Supertram "Sheffield Supertram"), [West Midlands Metro](/wiki/West_Midlands_Metro "West Midlands Metro"), [Nottingham Express Transit](/wiki/Nottingham_Express_Transit "Nottingham Express Transit"), and [Tramlink](/wiki/Tramlink "Tramlink") in South London.[[174]](#cite_note-pubwhite-180) England also has extensive domestic and international aviation links. The largest airport is [Heathrow](/wiki/Heathrow_Airport "Heathrow Airport"), which is the [world's second busiest airport measured by number of international passengers](/wiki/World%27s_busiest_airports_by_international_passenger_traffic "World's busiest airports by international passenger traffic").[[175]](#cite_note-wsj230211-181)

By sea there is ferry transport, both local and international, including from Liverpool to Ireland and the Isle of Man, and Hull to the Netherlands and Belgium.[[176]](#cite_note-waterworks-182) There are around 4,400 miles (7,100 km) of navigable waterways in England, half of which is owned by the [Canal & River Trust](/wiki/Canal_%26_River_Trust "Canal & River Trust"),[[176]](#cite_note-waterworks-182) however, water transport is very limited. The [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") is the major waterway in England, with imports and exports focused at the [Port of Tilbury](/wiki/Port_of_Tilbury "Port of Tilbury") in the [Thames Estuary](/wiki/Thames_Estuary "Thames Estuary"), one of the United Kingdom's three major ports.[[176]](#cite_note-waterworks-182)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Energy_in_the_United_Kingdom "Energy in the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Den_Brook_Wind_Farm_from_Main_Road.jpg/250px-Den_Brook_Wind_Farm_from_Main_Road.jpg)](/wiki/File:Den_Brook_Wind_Farm_from_Main_Road.jpg)

Wind turbines at [Den Brook](/wiki/Den_Brook_Wind_Farm "Den Brook Wind Farm"), Devon. The UK is [one of the best sites in Europe for wind energy](/wiki/Wind_power_in_the_United_Kingdom "Wind power in the United Kingdom"), and wind power production is its fastest growing supply.[[177]](#cite_note-:1-183)[[178]](#cite_note-Roadmap-184)

Successive governments have outlined numerous commitments to reduce [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") emissions. Notably, the UK is [one of the best sites in Europe for wind energy](/wiki/Wind_power_in_the_United_Kingdom "Wind power in the United Kingdom"), and wind power production is its fastest growing supply.[[178]](#cite_note-Roadmap-184) Wind power contributed 26.8% of UK electricity generation in 2022.[[179]](#cite_note-185) England is home to [Hornsea 2](/wiki/Hornsea_Wind_Farm "Hornsea Wind Farm"), the largest offshore wind farm in the world, situated in waters roughly 89 kilometres off the coast of Yorkshire.[[180]](#cite_note-186)

The [Climate Change Act 2008](/wiki/Climate_Change_Act_2008 "Climate Change Act 2008") was passed in Parliament with an overwhelming majority across political parties. It sets out emission reduction targets that the UK must comply with legally. It represents the first global legally binding [climate change mitigation](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation") target set by a country.[[181]](#cite_note-187) [UK government energy policy](/wiki/Energy_policy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Energy policy of the United Kingdom") aims to play a key role in limiting [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions"), while meeting energy demand. Shifting availabilities of resources and development of technologies also change the country's [energy mix](/wiki/Energy_mix "Energy mix") through changes in costs.[[182]](#cite_note-188)

The current energy policy is the responsibility of the [Department for Energy Security and Net Zero](/wiki/Department_for_Energy_Security_and_Net_Zero "Department for Energy Security and Net Zero") and [Secretary of State for Energy Security and Net Zero](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Energy_Security_and_Net_Zero "Secretary of State for Energy Security and Net Zero").[[183]](#cite_note-189) The [Minister of State for Business, Energy and Clean Growth](/wiki/Minister_of_State_for_Business,_Energy_and_Clean_Growth "Minister of State for Business, Energy and Clean Growth") is responsible for green finance, climate science and innovation, and low carbon generation.[[184]](#cite_note-190) In 2024, the United Kingdom was ranked 5 out of 180 countries in the [Environmental Performance Index](/wiki/Environmental_Performance_Index "Environmental Performance Index").[[185]](#cite_note-191) A law has been passed that [UK greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") will be [net zero](/wiki/Net_zero "Net zero") by 2050.[[186]](#cite_note-192)

## Healthcare

Main article: [Healthcare in England](/wiki/Healthcare_in_England "Healthcare in England")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Sir_W.H._Beveridge%2C_head-and-shoulders_portrait%2C_facing_left.jpg/250px-Sir_W.H._Beveridge%2C_head-and-shoulders_portrait%2C_facing_left.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_W.H._Beveridge,_head-and-shoulders_portrait,_facing_left.jpg)

[William Beveridge](/wiki/William_Beveridge "William Beveridge")'s 1942 report *Social Insurance and Allied Services* served as the basis for the post-[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state").

The [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service_(England) "National Health Service (England)") (NHS), is the publicly funded [healthcare system](/wiki/Healthcare_system "Healthcare system") responsible for providing the majority of healthcare in the country. The NHS began on 5 July 1948, putting into effect the provisions of the [National Health Service Act 1946](/wiki/National_Health_Service_Act_1946 "National Health Service Act 1946"). It was based on the findings of the [Beveridge Report](/wiki/Beveridge_Report "Beveridge Report"), prepared by the economist and social reformer, [William Beveridge](/wiki/William_Beveridge "William Beveridge").[[187]](#cite_note-193) The NHS is largely funded by general taxation and [National Insurance](/wiki/National_Insurance "National Insurance") payments;[[188]](#cite_note-194) it provides most of its services free at the point of use, although there are charges for some people for eye tests, dental care, prescriptions and aspects of personal care.[[189]](#cite_note-195)

The government department responsible for the NHS is the [Department of Health](/wiki/Department_of_Health_(United_Kingdom) "Department of Health (United Kingdom)"), under the [Secretary of State for Health](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Health "Secretary of State for Health"). Most of the department's expenses are on the NHS – £98.6 billion was spent in 2008–2009.[[190]](#cite_note-budget2008-196) Regulatory bodies such as the [General Medical Council](/wiki/General_Medical_Council "General Medical Council") and the [Nursing and Midwifery Council](/wiki/Nursing_and_Midwifery_Council "Nursing and Midwifery Council") are organised on a UK-wide basis, as are non-governmental bodies such as the [Royal Colleges](/wiki/Medical_royal_college "Medical royal college").

The average [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") is 77.5 years for males and 81.7 years for females, the highest of the four [countries of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom").[[191]](#cite_note-LifeExpect-197) The south of England has a higher life expectancy than the north, but regional differences seem to be slowly narrowing: between 1991–1993 and 2012–2014, life expectancy in the North East increased by 6.0 years and in the North West by 5.8 years.[[191]](#cite_note-LifeExpect-197)

## Demography

Main article: [Demographics of England](/wiki/Demographics_of_England "Demographics of England")

### Population

Main article: [English people](/wiki/English_people "English people")

See also: [English diaspora](/wiki/English_diaspora "English diaspora"), [Cornish people](/wiki/Cornish_people "Cornish people"), and [List of urban areas in the United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of urban areas in the United Kingdom")

[![Map of England with regions shaded in different shades of blue.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/England_counties_population_%28crop%29.png/250px-England_counties_population_%28crop%29.png)](/wiki/File:England_counties_population_(crop).png)

The [metropolitan](/wiki/Metropolitan_county "Metropolitan county") and [non-metropolitan counties](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county"), colour-coded to show population

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/England_and_Wales_population_cartogram_districts.svg/250px-England_and_Wales_population_cartogram_districts.svg.png)](/wiki/File:England_and_Wales_population_cartogram_districts.svg)

Population of England and Wales by administrative areas. Their size shows their population, with some approximation. Each group of squares in the map key is 20% of total number of districts.

With over 56 million inhabitants, England is by far the most populous country of the United Kingdom, accounting for 84% of the combined total.[[4]](#cite_note-ONS_mid-year_pop_est-8) England taken as a unit and measured against international states would be the 26th largest [country by population](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population "List of countries and dependencies by population") in the world.[[192]](#cite_note-198)

The [English people](/wiki/English_people "English people") are [British people](/wiki/British_people "British people").[[193]](#cite_note-ethnicityengl-199) There is an English diaspora in former parts of the British Empire; especially the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand").[[g]](#cite_note-204) Since the late 1990s, many English people [have migrated](/wiki/British_migration_to_Spain "British migration to Spain") to Spain.[[198]](#cite_note-205) Due in particular to the economic prosperity of [South East England](/wiki/South_East_England "South East England"), it has received many economic migrants from the other parts of the United Kingdom.[[193]](#cite_note-ethnicityengl-199) There has been [significant Irish migration](/wiki/Irish_migration_to_Great_Britain "Irish migration to Great Britain").[[199]](#cite_note-206) The proportion of ethnically European residents totals at 81.7%,[[200]](#cite_note-ethnicityengl-2021-207) including [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British"), [Germans](/wiki/British_Germans "British Germans")[[201]](#cite_note-migra-208) and [Poles](/wiki/Polish_British "Polish British"),[[193]](#cite_note-ethnicityengl-199) down from 94.1% in 1991.[[193]](#cite_note-ethnicityengl-199) Other people from much further afield in the former British colonies have arrived since the 1950s: in particular, about 7% of people living in England have family [origins in the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/South_Asians_in_the_United_Kingdom "South Asians in the United Kingdom"), mostly [India](/wiki/British_Indians "British Indians"), [Pakistan](/wiki/British_Pakistanis "British Pakistanis") and [Bangladesh](/wiki/British_Bangladeshis "British Bangladeshis").[[200]](#cite_note-ethnicityengl-2021-207)[[201]](#cite_note-migra-208) About 0.7% are [Chinese](/wiki/British_Chinese "British Chinese"),[[200]](#cite_note-ethnicityengl-2021-207)[[201]](#cite_note-migra-208) 0.6% are [Arabs](/wiki/British_Arabs "British Arabs").[[200]](#cite_note-ethnicityengl-2021-207) 4.0% of the population are [black](/wiki/Black_British_people "Black British people"), from Africa and the [Caribbean](/wiki/British_African-Caribbean_people "British African-Caribbean people"), especially former British colonies,[[200]](#cite_note-ethnicityengl-2021-207)[[201]](#cite_note-migra-208) and 2.9% identified as multiracial or [mixed](/wiki/Mixed_(United_Kingdom_ethnicity_category) "Mixed (United Kingdom ethnicity category)").[[200]](#cite_note-ethnicityengl-2021-207)

In 2007, 22% of primary school children in England were from [ethnic minority](/wiki/Minority_group "Minority group") families,[[202]](#cite_note-Paton-209) and in 2011 that figure was 26.5%.[[203]](#cite_note-Shepherd-210) About half of the population increase between 1991 and 2001 was due to immigration.[[204]](#cite_note-211)

England contains one indigenous national minority, the [Cornish people](/wiki/Cornish_people "Cornish people"), recognised by the UK government under the [Framework Convention for the Protection of National Minorities](/wiki/Framework_Convention_for_the_Protection_of_National_Minorities "Framework Convention for the Protection of National Minorities") in 2014.[[205]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-212)

### Language

Further information: [Languages of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Languages_of_the_United_Kingdom "Languages of the United Kingdom") and [English language in England](/wiki/English_language_in_England "English language in England")

| Language | Native speakers (thousands)[[206]](#cite_note-213) |
