[English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), today widely spoken around the world,[[207]](#cite_note-214) originated in what is now England, where it remains the principal tongue. According to a 2011 census, it is spoken well or very well by 98% of the population[[208]](#cite_note-CensusEnglish-215)

[English language learning and teaching](/wiki/English_as_a_second_or_foreign_language "English as a second or foreign language") is an important [economic activity](/wiki/Economics "Economics"). There is no [legislation](/wiki/United_Kingdom_legislation "United Kingdom legislation") mandating an [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") for England,[[209]](#cite_note-216) but English is the only language used for official business. Despite the country's relatively small size, there are many distinct [regional accents](/wiki/Regional_accents_of_English_speakers#England "Regional accents of English speakers").

Cornish died out as a community language in the 18th century but is being revived,[[210]](#cite_note-217) and is now protected under the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages").[[211]](#cite_note-218) It is spoken by 0.1% of people in [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"),[[212]](#cite_note-219) and is taught to some degree in several primary and secondary schools.[[213]](#cite_note-220)

[State schools](/wiki/State_school "State school") teach students a [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") or [third language](/wiki/Third_language "Third language") from the ages of seven, most commonly French, Spanish or German.[[214]](#cite_note-221) It was reported in 2007 that around 800,000 school students spoke a foreign language at home,[[202]](#cite_note-Paton-209) the most common being [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"). However, following the 2011 census data released by the [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics"), figures now show that Polish is the main language spoken in England after English.[[215]](#cite_note-222) In 2022, [British Sign Language](/wiki/British_Sign_Language "British Sign Language") became an official language of England when the [British Sign Language Act 2022](/wiki/British_Sign_Language_Act_2022 "British Sign Language Act 2022") came into effect.[[216]](#cite_note-223)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in England](/wiki/Religion_in_England "Religion in England")

Further information: [History of Christianity in England](/wiki/History_of_Christianity_in_England "History of Christianity in England")

In the 2021 census, 46.3% of the population of England specified their religion as Christian, 36.7% answered that they had no religion, 6.7% specified that they were [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim"), while 4.3% of the population belongs to other religions and 6% did not give an answer.[[217]](#cite_note-224) Christianity is the most widely practised religion in England. The [established church](/wiki/Established_church "Established church") of England is the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"),[[218]](#cite_note-225) which left communion with [Rome](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") in the 1530s when [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") was unable to annul his marriage to [Catherine of Aragon](/wiki/Catherine_of_Aragon "Catherine of Aragon"). The church regards itself as both Catholic and [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant").[[219]](#cite_note-226)

There are [High Church](/wiki/High_Church "High Church") and [Low Church](/wiki/Low_Church "Low Church") traditions and some Anglicans regard themselves as [Anglo-Catholics](/wiki/Anglo-Catholics "Anglo-Catholics"), following the [Tractarian movement](/wiki/Tractarian_movement "Tractarian movement"). The monarch of the United Kingdom is the [supreme governor of the Church of England](/wiki/Supreme_governor_of_the_Church_of_England "Supreme governor of the Church of England"), which has around 26 million baptised members (of whom the vast majority are not regular churchgoers). In 2016, research at [St. Mary's University](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_University,_Twickenham "St Mary's University, Twickenham") in London reported that 19.8% of the population of England and Wales identified as Anglican.[[220]](#cite_note-:3-227)[[221]](#cite_note-:4-228) It forms part of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion") with the [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury") acting as its symbolic worldwide head.[[222]](#cite_note-229) Many [cathedrals](/wiki/List_of_cathedrals_in_England "List of cathedrals in England") and parish churches are historic buildings of significant architectural importance, such as [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey"), [York Minster](/wiki/York_Minster "York Minster"), [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral"), and [Salisbury Cathedral](/wiki/Salisbury_Cathedral "Salisbury Cathedral").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Westminster_Abbey%2C_west_facade%2C_August_2014.jpg/250px-Westminster_Abbey%2C_west_facade%2C_August_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Westminster_Abbey,_west_facade,_August_2014.jpg)

[Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") is a notable example of [English Gothic architecture](/wiki/English_Gothic_architecture "English Gothic architecture"). The [coronation of the British monarch](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_British_monarch "Coronation of the British monarch") traditionally takes place at the Abbey.

The second-largest Christian denomination is the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). Since its reintroduction after the [Catholic Emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_Emancipation "Catholic Emancipation"), the Church has organised ecclesiastically on an [England and Wales](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church_in_England_and_Wales "Roman Catholic Church in England and Wales") basis where there are 4.5 million members (most of whom are English).[[223]](#cite_note-230) In 2016, 8.3% of the population of England and Wales identified as Catholic.[[220]](#cite_note-:3-227)[[221]](#cite_note-:4-228) There has been one Pope from England to date, [Adrian IV](/wiki/Pope_Adrian_IV "Pope Adrian IV"), while saints [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") and [Anselm](/wiki/Anselm_of_Canterbury "Anselm of Canterbury") are regarded as [Doctors of the Church](/wiki/Doctors_of_the_Church "Doctors of the Church").

A form of [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") known as [Methodism](/wiki/Methodist_Church_of_Great_Britain "Methodist Church of Great Britain") is the third largest Christian practice and grew out of Anglicanism through [John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley").[[224]](#cite_note-231) It gained popularity in the [mill towns](/wiki/Mill_town "Mill town") of [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire") and [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire"), and amongst tin miners in [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall").[[225]](#cite_note-232) There are other [non-conformist](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") minorities, such as [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists"), [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers"), [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church"), [Unitarians](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") and [The Salvation Army](/wiki/The_Salvation_Army "The Salvation Army").[[226]](#cite_note-233)

The patron saint of England is [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George"); his symbolic cross is included in the flag of England.[[227]](#cite_note-flaghistory-234) There are many other English and associated saints, including [Cuthbert](/wiki/Cuthbert "Cuthbert"), [Edmund](/wiki/Edmund_the_Martyr "Edmund the Martyr"), [Alban](/wiki/Saint_Alban "Saint Alban"), [Wilfrid](/wiki/Wilfrid "Wilfrid"), [Aidan](/wiki/Aidan_of_Lindisfarne "Aidan of Lindisfarne"), [Edward the Confessor](/wiki/Edward_the_Confessor "Edward the Confessor"), [John Fisher](/wiki/John_Fisher "John Fisher"), [Thomas More](/wiki/Thomas_More "Thomas More"), [Petroc](/wiki/Saint_Petroc "Saint Petroc"), [Piran](/wiki/Saint_Piran "Saint Piran"), [Margaret Clitherow](/wiki/Margaret_Clitherow "Margaret Clitherow") and [Thomas Becket](/wiki/Thomas_Becket "Thomas Becket"). There are non-Christian religions practised. [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") have a history of a small minority on the island since 1070.[[228]](#cite_note-jews-235) They were expelled from England in 1290 following the [Edict of Expulsion](/wiki/Edict_of_Expulsion "Edict of Expulsion"), and were allowed back in 1656.[[228]](#cite_note-jews-235)

Especially since the 1950s, religions from the [former British colonies](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") have grown in numbers, due to immigration. [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the most common of these, now accounting for around 5% of the population in England.[[229]](#cite_note-statsio-236) [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") are next in number, adding up to 2.8% combined,[[229]](#cite_note-statsio-236) introduced from India and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia").[[229]](#cite_note-statsio-236)

A small minority of the population practise ancient [Pagan religions](/wiki/Pagan_religion "Pagan religion"). [Neopaganism in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Neopaganism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Neopaganism in the United Kingdom") is primarily represented by [Wicca](/wiki/Wicca "Wicca") and [Neopagan witchcraft](/wiki/Neopagan_witchcraft "Neopagan witchcraft"), [Druidry](/wiki/Neo-Druidry "Neo-Druidry"), and [Heathenry](/wiki/Germanic_neopaganism "Germanic neopaganism"). According to the [2011 census](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Census_2011 "United Kingdom Census 2011"), there are roughly 53,172 people who identify as Pagan in England,[[h]](#cite_note-pagan-238) including 11,026 [Wiccans](/wiki/Wicca "Wicca").[[i]](#cite_note-239) 24.7% of people in England declared [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion"), compared with 14.6% in 2001.[[230]](#cite_note-2011_ONS_results-237) [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich") had the highest such proportion at 42.5%, followed by [Brighton and Hove](/wiki/Brighton_and_Hove "Brighton and Hove") at 42.4%.

## Education

Main article: [Education in England](/wiki/Education_in_England "Education in England")

The [Department for Education](/wiki/Department_for_Education "Department for Education") is the government department responsible for issues affecting people in England up to the age of 19, including education.[[231]](#cite_note-240) State-funded schools are attended by about 93% of English schoolchildren.[[232]](#cite_note-241) Education is the responsibility of the [Secretary of State for Education](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Education "Secretary of State for Education").[[233]](#cite_note-242)

Children between the ages of 3 and 5 attend nursery or an [Early Years Foundation Stage](/wiki/Early_Years_Foundation_Stage "Early Years Foundation Stage") reception unit within a primary school. Children between the ages of 5 and 11 attend primary school, and secondary school is attended by those aged between 11 and 16. State-funded schools are obliged by law to teach the [National Curriculum](/wiki/National_Curriculum_for_England "National Curriculum for England"); basic areas of learning include English literature, English language, mathematics, science, art & design, citizenship, history, geography, religious education, design & technology, computing, ancient & modern languages, music, and physical education.[[234]](#cite_note-243)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Radcliffe_Camera_by_Fenlio.jpg/250px-Radcliffe_Camera_by_Fenlio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Radcliffe_Camera_by_Fenlio.jpg)

The [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") was founded in 1096, making it the world's second-oldest university.

The [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") coordinated by the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") currently ranks the overall knowledge and skills of British 15-year-olds as 13th in the world in literacy, mathematics, and science with the average British student scoring 503.7, well above the OECD average of 493.[[235]](#cite_note-244)

Although most English secondary schools are comprehensive, there are selective intake [grammar schools](/wiki/Grammar_school "Grammar school") to which entrance is subject to passing the [eleven-plus](/wiki/Eleven-plus "Eleven-plus") exam. Around 7.2 per cent of English schoolchildren attend [private schools](/wiki/Independent_school_(UK) "Independent school (UK)"), which are funded by private sources.[[236]](#cite_note-245) Standards in state schools are monitored by the [Office for Standards in Education](/wiki/Office_for_Standards_in_Education "Office for Standards in Education"), and in private schools by the [Independent Schools Inspectorate](/wiki/Independent_Schools_Inspectorate "Independent Schools Inspectorate").[[237]](#cite_note-246)

After finishing compulsory education, students take [GCSE](/wiki/General_Certificate_of_Secondary_Education "General Certificate of Secondary Education") examinations. Students may then opt to continue into [further education](/wiki/Further_education "Further education") for two years. [Further education colleges](/wiki/List_of_further_education_colleges_in_England "List of further education colleges in England") (particularly [sixth form colleges](/wiki/Sixth_form_colleges "Sixth form colleges")) often form part of a secondary school site. [A-level](/wiki/A-level "A-level") examinations are sat by a large number of further education students, and often form the basis of an application to university. Further education covers a wide curriculum of study and [apprenticeships](/wiki/Apprenticeship "Apprenticeship"), including [T-levels](/wiki/T_Level "T Level"), [BTEC](/wiki/Business_and_Technology_Education_Council "Business and Technology Education Council"), [NVQ](/wiki/NVQ "NVQ") and others. [Tertiary colleges](/wiki/Tertiary_college "Tertiary college") provide both academic and vocational courses.[[238]](#cite_note-247)

### Higher education

Higher education students normally attend university from age 18 onwards, where they study for an [academic degree](/wiki/Academic_degree "Academic degree"). There are over 90 universities in England, all but one of which are [public institutions](/wiki/Public_university "Public university"). The [Department for Business, Innovation and Skills](/wiki/Department_for_Business,_Innovation_and_Skills "Department for Business, Innovation and Skills") is the government department responsible for higher education in England.[[239]](#cite_note-248) Students are generally entitled to [student loans](/wiki/Student_loan "Student loan") to cover [tuition fees](/wiki/Tuition_fees "Tuition fees") and living costs.[[j]](#cite_note-250) The first degree offered to undergraduates is the [bachelor's degree](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree"), which usually takes three years to complete. Students are then able to work towards a postgraduate degree, which usually takes one year, or a doctorate, which takes three or more years.[[241]](#cite_note-FHEQ-251)

[England's universities](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_England "List of universities in England") include some of the highest-ranked universities in the world. As of 2024, four England-based universities, the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"), [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"), [Imperial College London](/wiki/Imperial_College_London "Imperial College London"), and [University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London"), are ranked amongst the top ten in the 2024 *[QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings")*. The University of Cambridge, founded in 1209, and the University of Oxford, founded in 1096, are the two [oldest universities in the English-speaking world](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation").[[242]](#cite_note-252)

The [London School of Economics](/wiki/London_School_of_Economics "London School of Economics") has been described as the world's leading social science institution for both teaching and research.[[243]](#cite_note-london_156-253) The [London Business School](/wiki/London_Business_School "London Business School") is considered one of the world's leading business schools and in 2010 its MBA programme was ranked best in the world by the *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")*.[[244]](#cite_note-ft-254) [Academic degrees](/wiki/Academic_degree "Academic degree") in England are usually split into classes: first class, upper second class, lower second class, third, and unclassified.[[241]](#cite_note-FHEQ-251) [The King's School, Canterbury](/wiki/The_King%27s_School,_Canterbury "The King's School, Canterbury") and [King's School, Rochester](/wiki/King%27s_School,_Rochester "King's School, Rochester") are the oldest schools in the English-speaking world.[[245]](#cite_note-255) Many of England's most well-known schools, such as [Winchester College](/wiki/Winchester_College "Winchester College"), [Eton](/wiki/Eton_College "Eton College"), [St Paul's School](/wiki/St_Paul%27s_School_(London) "St Paul's School (London)"), [Harrow School](/wiki/Harrow_School "Harrow School") and [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School") are fee-paying institutions.[[246]](#cite_note-256)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of England](/wiki/Culture_of_England "Culture of England")

Further information: [English Renaissance](/wiki/English_Renaissance "English Renaissance")

### Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of England](/wiki/Architecture_of_England "Architecture of England")

Many ancient [standing stone](/wiki/Standing_stone "Standing stone") monuments were erected during the prehistoric period; amongst the best known are [Stonehenge](/wiki/Stonehenge "Stonehenge"), [Devil's Arrows](/wiki/Devil%27s_Arrows "Devil's Arrows"), [Rudston Monolith](/wiki/Rudston_Monolith "Rudston Monolith") and [Castlerigg](/wiki/Castlerigg_stone_circle "Castlerigg stone circle").[[247]](#cite_note-257) With the introduction of [Ancient Roman architecture](/wiki/Ancient_Roman_architecture "Ancient Roman architecture") there was a development of [basilicas](/wiki/Basilicas "Basilicas"), [baths](/wiki/Roman_baths "Roman baths"), [amphitheaters](/wiki/Amphitheaters "Amphitheaters"), [triumphal arches](/wiki/Triumphal_arch "Triumphal arch"), [villas](/wiki/Villa "Villa"), [Roman temples](/wiki/Roman_temple "Roman temple"), [Roman roads](/wiki/Roman_road "Roman road"), [Roman forts](/wiki/Roman_fort "Roman fort"), [stockades](/wiki/Stockade "Stockade") and [aqueducts](/wiki/Aqueduct_(bridge) "Aqueduct (bridge)").[[248]](#cite_note-roman-258) It was the Romans who founded the first cities and towns such as London, Bath, York, Chester and St Albans. Perhaps the best-known example is [Hadrian's Wall](/wiki/Hadrian%27s_Wall "Hadrian's Wall") stretching right across northern England.[[248]](#cite_note-roman-258) Another well-preserved example is the [Roman Baths](/wiki/Roman_Baths_(Bath) "Roman Baths (Bath)") at [Bath, Somerset](/wiki/Bath,_Somerset "Bath, Somerset").[[248]](#cite_note-roman-258)

[![A castle of square plan surrounded by a water-filled moat. It has round corner towers and a forbidding appearance.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Bodiam-castle-10My8-1197.jpg/250px-Bodiam-castle-10My8-1197.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bodiam-castle-10My8-1197.jpg)

[Bodiam Castle](/wiki/Bodiam_Castle "Bodiam Castle") is a 14th-century [moated](/wiki/Moat "Moat") [castle](/wiki/Castle "Castle") near [Robertsbridge](/wiki/Robertsbridge "Robertsbridge") in East Sussex.

[Early medieval architecture](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_architecture "Anglo-Saxon architecture")'s secular buildings were simple constructions mainly using timber with [thatch](/wiki/Thatch "Thatch") for roofing. Ecclesiastical architecture ranged from a synthesis of [Hiberno](/wiki/Early_Christian_Ireland "Early Christian Ireland")–[Saxon](/wiki/Germanic_Christianity "Germanic Christianity") [monasticism](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism"),[[249]](#cite_note-259)[[250]](#cite_note-260) to [Early Christian](/wiki/Early_Christian_art_and_architecture "Early Christian art and architecture") [basilica](/wiki/Basilica "Basilica") and architecture characterised by pilaster-strips, blank arcading, baluster shafts and triangular headed openings. After the Norman conquest in 1066 various [castles](/wiki/Castles_in_England "Castles in England") were created; the best known include the [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London"), [Warwick Castle](/wiki/Warwick_Castle "Warwick Castle"), [Durham Castle](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle") and [Windsor Castle](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle").[[251]](#cite_note-buildings-261)

Throughout the Plantagenet era, an [English Gothic architecture](/wiki/English_Gothic_architecture "English Gothic architecture") flourished, with prime examples including the [medieval cathedrals](/wiki/Architecture_of_the_medieval_cathedrals_of_England "Architecture of the medieval cathedrals of England") such as [Canterbury Cathedral](/wiki/Canterbury_Cathedral "Canterbury Cathedral"), [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") and [York Minster](/wiki/York_Minster "York Minster").[[251]](#cite_note-buildings-261) Expanding on the [Norman base](/wiki/Norman_architecture "Norman architecture") there was also [castles](/wiki/Castle "Castle"), [palaces](/wiki/Palace "Palace"), [great houses](/wiki/List_of_historic_houses_in_England "List of historic houses in England"), universities and [parish churches](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church"). Medieval architecture was completed with the 16th-century [Tudor style](/wiki/Tudor_architecture "Tudor architecture"); the four-centred arch, now known as the [Tudor arch](/wiki/Tudor_arch "Tudor arch"), was a defining feature as were [wattle and daub](/wiki/Wattle_and_daub "Wattle and daub") houses domestically. In the aftermath of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") a form of architecture echoing classical antiquity synthesised with Christianity appeared, the [English Baroque](/wiki/English_Baroque "English Baroque") style of architect [Christopher Wren](/wiki/Christopher_Wren "Christopher Wren") being particularly championed.[[252]](#cite_note-262)

[Georgian architecture](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") followed in a more refined style, evoking a simple Palladian form; the [Royal Crescent](/wiki/Royal_Crescent "Royal Crescent") at Bath is one of the best examples of this. With the emergence of [romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") during Victorian period, a [Gothic Revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival "Gothic Revival") was launched. In addition to this, around the same time the Industrial Revolution paved the way for buildings such as [The Crystal Palace](/wiki/The_Crystal_Palace "The Crystal Palace"). Since the 1930s various [modernist](/wiki/Modernist_architecture "Modernist architecture") forms have appeared whose reception is often controversial, though traditionalist resistance movements continue with support in influential places.[[k]](#cite_note-264)

### Gardens

Main article: [English garden](/wiki/English_garden "English garden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Stourhead_lake_in_midsummer_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Stourhead_lake_in_midsummer_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stourhead_lake_in_midsummer_-_panoramio.jpg)

The landscape garden at Stourhead. Inspired by the great [landscape artists](/wiki/Landscape_artists "Landscape artists") of the seventeenth century, the landscape garden was described as a "living work of art" when first opened in the 1750s.[[254]](#cite_note-265)

Landscape gardening, as developed by [Capability Brown](/wiki/Capability_Brown "Capability Brown"), set an international trend for the [English landscape garden](/wiki/English_garden "English garden"). Gardening, and visiting gardens, are regarded as typically English pursuits. The English garden presented an idealised view of nature. At large country houses, the English garden usually included lakes, sweeps of gently rolling lawns set against groves of trees, and recreations of classical temples, [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") ruins, bridges, and other picturesque architecture, designed to recreate an idyllic pastoral landscape.[[255]](#cite_note-:2-266)

By the end of the 18th century, the English garden was being imitated by the [French landscape garden](/wiki/French_landscape_garden "French landscape garden"), and as far away as [Pavlovsk, Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Pavlovsk,_Saint_Petersburg "Pavlovsk, Saint Petersburg"), the gardens of the future [Emperor Paul](/wiki/Emperor_Paul "Emperor Paul"). It also had a major influence on the [public parks](/wiki/Public_parks "Public parks") and gardens which appeared around the world in the 19th century.[[256]](#cite_note-267) The English landscape garden was centred on the [English country house](/wiki/English_country_house "English country house") and manor houses.[[255]](#cite_note-:2-266)

[English Heritage](/wiki/English_Heritage "English Heritage") and the [National Trust](/wiki/National_Trust "National Trust") preserve great gardens and landscape parks throughout the country.[[257]](#cite_note-268) The [RHS Chelsea Flower Show](/wiki/RHS_Chelsea_Flower_Show "RHS Chelsea Flower Show") is held every year by the [Royal Horticultural Society](/wiki/Royal_Horticultural_Society "Royal Horticultural Society") and is said to be the largest gardening show in the world.[[258]](#cite_note-269)

### Folklore

Main article: [English folklore](/wiki/English_folklore "English folklore")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/The_King_joins_the_hands_of_Robin_Hood_and_Maid_Marian.jpg/250px-The_King_joins_the_hands_of_Robin_Hood_and_Maid_Marian.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_King_joins_the_hands_of_Robin_Hood_and_Maid_Marian.jpg)

[Robin Hood](/wiki/Robin_Hood "Robin Hood") and [Maid Marian](/wiki/Maid_Marian "Maid Marian") with [Richard I of England](/wiki/Richard_I_of_England "Richard I of England")

English folklore developed over many centuries. Some of the characters and stories are present across England, but most belong to specific regions. Common folkloric beings include [pixies](/wiki/Pixie "Pixie"), [giants](/wiki/Giant_(mythology) "Giant (mythology)"), [elves](/wiki/Elf "Elf"), [bogeymen](/wiki/Bogeymen "Bogeymen"), [trolls](/wiki/Troll "Troll"), [goblins](/wiki/Goblin "Goblin") and [dwarves](/wiki/Dwarf_(mythology) "Dwarf (mythology)"). While many legends and folk-customs are thought to be ancient, such as the tales featuring [Offa of Angel](/wiki/Offa_of_Angel "Offa of Angel") and [Wayland the Smith](/wiki/Wayland_the_Smith "Wayland the Smith"),[[259]](#cite_note-keary-270) others date from after the Norman invasion. The legends featuring [Robin Hood](/wiki/Robin_Hood "Robin Hood") and his [Merry Men](/wiki/Merry_Men "Merry Men") of [Sherwood](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest"), and their battles with the [Sheriff of Nottingham](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Nottingham "Sheriff of Nottingham"), are amongst the best-known of these.[[260]](#cite_note-271)

During the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages") tales originating from Brythonic traditions entered English folklore and developed into the [Arthurian myth](/wiki/Arthurian_myth "Arthurian myth").[[261]](#cite_note-woodbbc-272)[[262]](#cite_note-higham1-273)[[263]](#cite_note-274) These were derived from [Anglo-Norman](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_language "Anglo-Norman language"), Welsh and French sources,[[262]](#cite_note-higham1-273) featuring [King Arthur](/wiki/King_Arthur "King Arthur"), [Camelot](/wiki/Camelot "Camelot"), [Excalibur](/wiki/Excalibur "Excalibur"), [Merlin](/wiki/Merlin "Merlin") and the [Knights of the Round Table](/wiki/Knights_of_the_Round_Table "Knights of the Round Table") such as [Lancelot](/wiki/Lancelot "Lancelot"). These stories are most centrally brought together within [Geoffrey of Monmouth](/wiki/Geoffrey_of_Monmouth "Geoffrey of Monmouth")'s *[Historia Regum Britanniae](/wiki/Historia_Regum_Britanniae "Historia Regum Britanniae")* (*History of the Kings of Britain*).[[l]](#cite_note-276)

Some folk figures are based on semi or actual historical people whose story has been passed down centuries.[[265]](#cite_note-277) On 5 November people celebrate [Bonfire Night](/wiki/Bonfire_Night "Bonfire Night") to commemorate the foiling of the [Gunpowder Plot](/wiki/Gunpowder_Plot "Gunpowder Plot") centred on [Guy Fawkes](/wiki/Guy_Fawkes "Guy Fawkes"). There are various national and regional folk activities, participated in to this day, such as [Morris dancing](/wiki/Morris_dancing "Morris dancing"), [Maypole dancing](/wiki/Maypole_dance "Maypole dance"), [Rapper sword](/wiki/Rapper_sword "Rapper sword") in the North East, [Long Sword dance](/wiki/Long_Sword_dance "Long Sword dance") in Yorkshire, [Mummers Plays](/wiki/Mummers_Play "Mummers Play"), [bottle-kicking](/wiki/Bottle-kicking "Bottle-kicking") in Leicestershire, and [cheese-rolling](/wiki/Cooper%27s_Hill_Cheese-Rolling_and_Wake "Cooper's Hill Cheese-Rolling and Wake") at [Cooper's Hill](/wiki/Brockworth,_Gloucestershire "Brockworth, Gloucestershire").[[266]](#cite_note-278) There is no official national costume, but a few are well established such as the [Pearly Kings and Queens](/wiki/Pearly_Kings_and_Queens "Pearly Kings and Queens") associated with cockneys, the [Royal Guard](/wiki/Queen%27s_Guard "Queen's Guard"), the [Morris costume](/wiki/Morris_dance "Morris dance") and [Beefeaters](/wiki/Beefeater "Beefeater").[[267]](#cite_note-279)

### Cuisine

Main article: [English cuisine](/wiki/English_cuisine "English cuisine")

Since the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period") the food of England has historically been characterised by its simplicity of approach and a reliance on the high quality of natural produce.[[268]](#cite_note-280) During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and the Renaissance, English cuisine enjoyed an excellent reputation, though a decline began during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") with increasing urbanisation. The cuisine of England has, however, recently undergone a revival, which has been recognised by food critics with some good ratings in *[Restaurant](/wiki/Restaurant_(magazine) "Restaurant (magazine)")*'s [best restaurant in the world](/wiki/Best_restaurant_in_the_world "Best restaurant in the world") charts.[[269]](#cite_note-281)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/English-cusine-exports.jpg/250px-English-cusine-exports.jpg)](/wiki/File:English-cusine-exports.jpg)

English foods: clockwise from top left—[tea cakes](/wiki/Tea_cakes "Tea cakes"), [cheeses](/wiki/English_cheese "English cheese"), [wines](/wiki/English_wine "English wine") and [cider](/wiki/Cider "Cider")

Traditional examples of English food include the [Sunday roast](/wiki/Sunday_roast "Sunday roast"), featuring a [roasted joint](/wiki/Roasting "Roasting") (usually beef, [lamb](/wiki/Lamb_and_mutton "Lamb and mutton"), chicken or pork) served with assorted vegetables, [Yorkshire pudding](/wiki/Yorkshire_pudding "Yorkshire pudding") and [gravy](/wiki/Gravy "Gravy").[[270]](#cite_note-tradfood-282) Other prominent meals include [fish and chips](/wiki/Fish_and_chips "Fish and chips") and the [full English breakfast](/wiki/Full_breakfast "Full breakfast") (generally consisting of [bacon](/wiki/Bacon "Bacon"), [sausages](/wiki/Sausages "Sausages"), grilled tomatoes, fried bread, [black pudding](/wiki/Black_pudding "Black pudding"), [baked beans](/wiki/Baked_beans "Baked beans"), [mushrooms](/wiki/Edible_mushroom "Edible mushroom") and eggs).[[271]](#cite_note-283) Various [meat pies](/wiki/Meat_pie "Meat pie") are consumed, such as [steak and kidney pie](/wiki/Steak_and_kidney_pie "Steak and kidney pie"), [steak and ale pie](/wiki/Steak_and_ale_pie "Steak and ale pie"), [cottage pie](/wiki/Cottage_pie "Cottage pie"), [pork pie](/wiki/Pork_pie "Pork pie") (usually eaten cold)[[270]](#cite_note-tradfood-282) and the [Cornish pasty](/wiki/Cornish_pasty "Cornish pasty").

Sausages are commonly eaten, either as [bangers and mash](/wiki/Bangers_and_mash "Bangers and mash") or [toad in the hole](/wiki/Toad_in_the_hole "Toad in the hole"). [Lancashire hotpot](/wiki/Lancashire_hotpot "Lancashire hotpot") is a well-known stew originating in the northwest. Some of the more popular cheeses are [Cheddar](/wiki/Cheddar_cheese "Cheddar cheese"), [Red Leicester](/wiki/Red_Leicester "Red Leicester"), [Wensleydale](/wiki/Wensleydale_(cheese) "Wensleydale (cheese)"), [Double Gloucester](/wiki/Double_Gloucester "Double Gloucester") and [Blue Stilton](/wiki/Stilton_cheese "Stilton cheese"). Many [Anglo-Indian](/wiki/Anglo-Indian "Anglo-Indian") hybrid dishes, [curries](/wiki/Curries "Curries"), have been created, such as [chicken tikka masala](/wiki/Chicken_tikka_masala "Chicken tikka masala") and [balti](/wiki/Balti_(food) "Balti (food)"). Traditional English dessert dishes include [apple pie](/wiki/Apple_pie "Apple pie") or other fruit pies; [spotted dick](/wiki/Spotted_dick "Spotted dick") – all generally served with [custard](/wiki/Custard "Custard"); and, more recently, [sticky toffee pudding](/wiki/Sticky_toffee_pudding "Sticky toffee pudding"). Sweet pastries include [scones](/wiki/Scones "Scones") served with jam or cream, dried fruit loaves, [Eccles cakes](/wiki/Eccles_cake "Eccles cake") and [mince pies](/wiki/Mince_pie "Mince pie") as well as sweet or spiced biscuits.

Common non-alcoholic drinks include tea[[272]](#cite_note-284) and coffee; frequently consumed alcoholic drinks include wine, [ciders](/wiki/Cider "Cider") and [English beers](/wiki/English_beer "English beer"), such as [bitter](/wiki/Bitter_(beer) "Bitter (beer)"), [mild](/wiki/Mild_ale "Mild ale"), [stout](/wiki/Stout "Stout") and [brown ale](/wiki/Brown_ale "Brown ale").[[273]](#cite_note-285)

### Visual arts

Main article: [English art](/wiki/English_art "English art")

See also: [Arts Council England](/wiki/Arts_Council_England "Arts Council England")

[![A horse-drawn wagon crossing a river towards a cottage, with trees and fields beyond](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/John_Constable_The_Hay_Wain.jpg/250px-John_Constable_The_Hay_Wain.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Constable_The_Hay_Wain.jpg)

*[The Hay Wain](/wiki/The_Hay_Wain "The Hay Wain")* by [John Constable](/wiki/John_Constable "John Constable"), 1821, is an archetypal English painting.

[![A painting of a red haired woman, sitting in a boat, surrounded by trees](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/John_William_Waterhouse_-_The_Lady_of_Shalott_-_Google_Art_Project_edit.jpg/250px-John_William_Waterhouse_-_The_Lady_of_Shalott_-_Google_Art_Project_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_William_Waterhouse_-_The_Lady_of_Shalott_-_Google_Art_Project_edit.jpg)

*[The Lady of Shalott](/wiki/The_Lady_of_Shalott_(painting) "The Lady of Shalott (painting)")* by [John William Waterhouse](/wiki/John_William_Waterhouse "John William Waterhouse"), 1888, in the [Pre-Raphaelite](/wiki/Pre-Raphaelite "Pre-Raphaelite") style

The earliest known examples are the prehistoric rock and [cave art](/wiki/Cave_art "Cave art") pieces, most prominent in [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire"), Northumberland and [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria"), but also feature further south, for example at [Creswell Crags](/wiki/Creswell_Crags "Creswell Crags").[[274]](#cite_note-286) With the arrival of [Roman culture](/wiki/Roman_culture "Roman culture") in the 1st century, various forms of art such as statues, busts, glasswork and mosaics were the norm. There are numerous surviving artefacts, such as those at [Lullingstone](/wiki/Lullingstone_Roman_Villa "Lullingstone Roman Villa") and [Aldborough](/wiki/Isurium_Brigantum "Isurium Brigantum").[[275]](#cite_note-287) During the Early Middle Ages the style favoured sculpted crosses and ivories, manuscript painting, gold and enamel jewellery, demonstrating a love of intricate, interwoven designs such as in the [Staffordshire Hoard](/wiki/Staffordshire_Hoard "Staffordshire Hoard") discovered in 2009. Some of these blended [Gaelic and Anglian](/wiki/Insular_art "Insular art") styles, such as the [Lindisfarne Gospels](/wiki/Lindisfarne_Gospels "Lindisfarne Gospels") and [Vespasian Psalter](/wiki/Vespasian_Psalter "Vespasian Psalter").[[276]](#cite_note-288) Later [Gothic art](/wiki/Gothic_art "Gothic art") was popular at Winchester and Canterbury, examples survive such as [Benedictional of St. Æthelwold](/wiki/Benedictional_of_St._%C3%86thelwold "Benedictional of St. Æthelwold") and [Luttrell Psalter](/wiki/Luttrell_Psalter "Luttrell Psalter").[[277]](#cite_note-engart-289)

The Tudor era saw [prominent artists](/wiki/Artists_of_the_Tudor_court "Artists of the Tudor court") as part of their court; portrait painting, which would remain an enduring part of English art, was boosted by German [Hans Holbein](/wiki/Hans_Holbein_the_Younger "Hans Holbein the Younger"), and natives such as [Nicholas Hilliard](/wiki/Nicholas_Hilliard "Nicholas Hilliard") built on this.[[277]](#cite_note-engart-289) Under the Stuarts, Continental artists were influential especially the Flemish, examples from the period include [Anthony van Dyck](/wiki/Anthony_van_Dyck "Anthony van Dyck"), [Peter Lely](/wiki/Peter_Lely "Peter Lely"), [Godfrey Kneller](/wiki/Godfrey_Kneller "Godfrey Kneller") and [William Dobson](/wiki/William_Dobson "William Dobson").[[277]](#cite_note-engart-289) The 18th century saw the founding of the [Royal Academy](/wiki/Royal_Academy "Royal Academy"); a [classicism](/wiki/Classicism "Classicism") based on the [High Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_art "Renaissance art") prevailed, with [Thomas Gainsborough](/wiki/Thomas_Gainsborough "Thomas Gainsborough") and [Joshua Reynolds](/wiki/Joshua_Reynolds "Joshua Reynolds") becoming two of England's most treasured artists.[[277]](#cite_note-engart-289)

In the 19th century, [John Constable](/wiki/John_Constable "John Constable") and [J. M. W. Turner](/wiki/J._M._W._Turner "J. M. W. Turner") were major landscape artists. The [Norwich School](/wiki/Norwich_School_(art_movement) "Norwich School (art movement)") continued the landscape tradition, while the [Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood](/wiki/Pre-Raphaelite_Brotherhood "Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood"), led by artists such as [Holman Hunt](/wiki/Holman_Hunt "Holman Hunt"), [Dante Gabriel Rossetti](/wiki/Dante_Gabriel_Rossetti "Dante Gabriel Rossetti") and [John Everett Millais](/wiki/John_Everett_Millais "John Everett Millais"), revived the [Early Renaissance](/wiki/Early_Renaissance "Early Renaissance") style with their vivid and detailed style.[[277]](#cite_note-engart-289) Prominent amongst 20th-century artists was [Henry Moore](/wiki/Henry_Moore "Henry Moore"), regarded as the voice of British sculpture, and of British modernism in general.[[278]](#cite_note-290) The [Royal Society of Arts](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_Arts "Royal Society of Arts") is an organisation committed to the arts.[[279]](#cite_note-291)

### Literature, poetry, and philosophy

Main articles: [English literature](/wiki/English_literature "English literature") and [English drama](/wiki/English_drama "English drama")

[![A man dressed in grey with a beard, holding a rosary, depicted next to a coat of arms.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Geoffrey_Chaucer_%2817th_century%29.jpg/250px-Geoffrey_Chaucer_%2817th_century%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Geoffrey_Chaucer_(17th_century).jpg)

[Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer") was an English author, poet and philosopher, best remembered for his unfinished frame narrative *[The Canterbury Tales](/wiki/The_Canterbury_Tales "The Canterbury Tales")*.

Early authors such as [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") and [Alcuin](/wiki/Alcuin "Alcuin") wrote in Latin.[[280]](#cite_note-warnancmod-292) The period of [Old English literature](/wiki/Old_English_literature "Old English literature") provided the epic poem *[Beowulf](/wiki/Beowulf "Beowulf")* and the secular prose of the *[Anglo-Saxon Chronicle](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle")*,[[281]](#cite_note-293) along with Christian writings such as *[Judith](/wiki/Judith_(poem) "Judith (poem)")*, [Cædmon](/wiki/C%C3%A6dmon "Cædmon")'s *[Hymn](/wiki/C%C3%A6dmon "Cædmon")* and [hagiographies](/wiki/Hagiography "Hagiography").[[280]](#cite_note-warnancmod-292) Following the Norman conquest [Latin](/wiki/Latin_literature "Latin literature") continued amongst the educated classes, as well as an [Anglo-Norman literature](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_literature "Anglo-Norman literature").

[Middle English literature](/wiki/Middle_English_literature "Middle English literature") emerged with [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer"), author of *[The Canterbury Tales](/wiki/The_Canterbury_Tales "The Canterbury Tales")*, along with [Gower](/wiki/John_Gower "John Gower"), the [Pearl Poet](/wiki/Pearl_Poet "Pearl Poet") and [Langland](/wiki/William_Langland "William Langland"). [William of Ockham](/wiki/William_of_Ockham "William of Ockham") and [Roger Bacon](/wiki/Roger_Bacon "Roger Bacon"), who were [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans"), were major philosophers of the Middle Ages. [Julian of Norwich](/wiki/Julian_of_Norwich "Julian of Norwich"), who wrote *[Revelations of Divine Love](/wiki/Revelations_of_Divine_Love "Revelations of Divine Love")*, was a prominent Christian mystic. With the [English Renaissance](/wiki/English_Renaissance "English Renaissance") literature in the [Early Modern English](/wiki/Early_Modern_English "Early Modern English") style appeared. [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare"), whose works include *[Hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet "Hamlet")*, *[Romeo and Juliet](/wiki/Romeo_and_Juliet "Romeo and Juliet")*, *[Macbeth](/wiki/Macbeth "Macbeth")*, and *[A Midsummer Night's Dream](/wiki/A_Midsummer_Night%27s_Dream "A Midsummer Night's Dream")*, remains one of the most championed authors in English literature.[[282]](#cite_note-294)

[Christopher Marlowe](/wiki/Christopher_Marlowe "Christopher Marlowe"), [Edmund Spenser](/wiki/Edmund_Spenser "Edmund Spenser"), [Philip Sydney](/wiki/Philip_Sydney "Philip Sydney"), [Thomas Kyd](/wiki/Thomas_Kyd "Thomas Kyd"), [John Donne](/wiki/John_Donne "John Donne"), and [Ben Jonson](/wiki/Ben_Jonson "Ben Jonson") are other established authors of the [Elizabethan age](/wiki/Elizabethan_literature "Elizabethan literature").[[283]](#cite_note-elizren-295) [Francis Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon "Francis Bacon") and [Thomas Hobbes](/wiki/Thomas_Hobbes "Thomas Hobbes") wrote on [empiricism](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism") and [materialism](/wiki/Materialism "Materialism"), including [scientific method](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method") and [social contract](/wiki/Social_contract "Social contract").[[283]](#cite_note-elizren-295) [Filmer](/wiki/Robert_Filmer "Robert Filmer") wrote on the [Divine Right of Kings](/wiki/Divine_Right_of_Kings "Divine Right of Kings"). [Marvell](/wiki/Andrew_Marvell "Andrew Marvell") was the best-known poet of the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England"),[[284]](#cite_note-296) while [John Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton") authored *[Paradise Lost](/wiki/Paradise_Lost "Paradise Lost")* during the [Restoration](/wiki/Restoration_literature "Restoration literature").

> This royal throne of kings, this sceptred isle, this earth of majesty, this seat of Mars, this other Eden, demi-paradise; this fortress, built by nature for herself. This blessed plot, this earth, this realm, this England.

[William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare").[[285]](#cite_note-297)

Some of the most prominent philosophers of the [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") were [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), [Thomas Paine](/wiki/Thomas_Paine "Thomas Paine"), [Samuel Johnson](/wiki/Samuel_Johnson "Samuel Johnson") and [Jeremy Bentham](/wiki/Jeremy_Bentham "Jeremy Bentham"). More radical elements were later countered by [Edmund Burke](/wiki/Edmund_Burke "Edmund Burke") who is regarded as the founder of conservatism.[[286]](#cite_note-298) The poet [Alexander Pope](/wiki/Alexander_Pope "Alexander Pope") with his satirical verse became well regarded. The English played a significant role in [romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism"): [Samuel Taylor Coleridge](/wiki/Samuel_Taylor_Coleridge "Samuel Taylor Coleridge"), [Lord Byron](/wiki/Lord_Byron "Lord Byron"), [John Keats](/wiki/John_Keats "John Keats"), [Mary Shelley](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley"), [Percy Bysshe Shelley](/wiki/Percy_Bysshe_Shelley "Percy Bysshe Shelley"), [William Blake](/wiki/William_Blake "William Blake") and [William Wordsworth](/wiki/William_Wordsworth "William Wordsworth") were major figures.[[287]](#cite_note-299)

In response to the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), agrarian writers sought a way between [liberty](/wiki/Liberty "Liberty") and tradition; [William Cobbett](/wiki/William_Cobbett "William Cobbett"), [G. K. Chesterton](/wiki/G._K._Chesterton "G. K. Chesterton") and [Hilaire Belloc](/wiki/Hilaire_Belloc "Hilaire Belloc") were main exponents, while the founder of [guild socialism](/wiki/Guild_socialism "Guild socialism"), [Arthur Penty](/wiki/Arthur_Penty "Arthur Penty"), and [cooperative movement](/wiki/Cooperative_movement "Cooperative movement") advocate [G. D. H. Cole](/wiki/G._D._H._Cole "G. D. H. Cole") are somewhat related.[[288]](#cite_note-300) Empiricism continued through [John Stuart Mill](/wiki/John_Stuart_Mill "John Stuart Mill") and [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell"), while [Bernard Williams](/wiki/Bernard_Williams "Bernard Williams") was involved in [analytics](/wiki/Analytics "Analytics"). Authors from around the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") include [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens"), the [Brontë sisters](/wiki/Bront%C3%AB_sisters "Brontë sisters"), [Jane Austen](/wiki/Jane_Austen "Jane Austen"), [George Eliot](/wiki/George_Eliot "George Eliot"), [Rudyard Kipling](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling"), [Thomas Hardy](/wiki/Thomas_Hardy "Thomas Hardy"), [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells") and [Lewis Carroll](/wiki/Lewis_Carroll "Lewis Carroll").[[289]](#cite_note-301) Since then England has continued to produce novelists such as [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell"), [D. H. Lawrence](/wiki/D._H._Lawrence "D. H. Lawrence"), [Virginia Woolf](/wiki/Virginia_Woolf "Virginia Woolf"), [C. S. Lewis](/wiki/C._S._Lewis "C. S. Lewis"), [Enid Blyton](/wiki/Enid_Blyton "Enid Blyton"), [Aldous Huxley](/wiki/Aldous_Huxley "Aldous Huxley"), [Agatha Christie](/wiki/Agatha_Christie "Agatha Christie"), [Terry Pratchett](/wiki/Terry_Pratchett "Terry Pratchett"), [J. R. R. Tolkien](/wiki/J._R._R._Tolkien "J. R. R. Tolkien"), and [J. K. Rowling](/wiki/J._K._Rowling "J. K. Rowling").[[290]](#cite_note-302)

### Performing arts

Further information: [Folk music of England](/wiki/Folk_music_of_England "Folk music of England")

See also: [Music of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Music_of_the_United_Kingdom "Music of the United Kingdom")
