The traditional [folk music of England](/wiki/Folk_music_of_England "Folk music of England") is centuries old and has contributed to several genres prominently; mostly [sea shanties](/wiki/Sea_shanties "Sea shanties"), [jigs](/wiki/Jig "Jig"), [hornpipes](/wiki/Hornpipe "Hornpipe") and [dance music](/wiki/Dance_music "Dance music"). It has its own distinct variations and regional peculiarities. Ballads featuring Robin Hood, printed by [Wynkyn de Worde](/wiki/Wynkyn_de_Worde "Wynkyn de Worde") in the 16th century, are an important artefact, as are [John Playford](/wiki/John_Playford "John Playford")'s *[The Dancing Master](/wiki/The_Dancing_Master "The Dancing Master")* and [Robert Harley](/wiki/Robert_Harley,_1st_Earl_of_Oxford_and_Earl_Mortimer "Robert Harley, 1st Earl of Oxford and Earl Mortimer")'s *[Roxburghe Ballads](/wiki/Roxburghe_Ballads "Roxburghe Ballads")* collections.[[291]](#cite_note-303) Some of the best-known songs are *[Greensleeves](/wiki/Greensleeves "Greensleeves")*, *[Pastime with Good Company](/wiki/Pastime_with_Good_Company "Pastime with Good Company")*, *[Maggie May](/wiki/Maggie_May_(folk_song) "Maggie May (folk song)")* and *[Spanish Ladies](/wiki/Spanish_Ladies "Spanish Ladies")* amongst others. Many [nursery rhymes](/wiki/Nursery_rhymes "Nursery rhymes") are of English origin such as *[Mary, Mary, Quite Contrary](/wiki/Mary,_Mary,_Quite_Contrary "Mary, Mary, Quite Contrary")*, *[Roses Are Red](/wiki/Roses_Are_Red "Roses Are Red")*, *[Jack and Jill](/wiki/Jack_and_Jill_(nursery_rhyme) "Jack and Jill (nursery rhyme)")*, *[London Bridge Is Falling Down](/wiki/London_Bridge_Is_Falling_Down "London Bridge Is Falling Down"), [The Grand Old Duke of York](/wiki/The_Grand_Old_Duke_of_York "The Grand Old Duke of York"), [Hey Diddle Diddle](/wiki/Hey_Diddle_Diddle "Hey Diddle Diddle")* and *[Humpty Dumpty](/wiki/Humpty_Dumpty "Humpty Dumpty")*.[[292]](#cite_note-304) Traditional English Christmas carols include "[We Wish You a Merry Christmas](/wiki/We_Wish_You_a_Merry_Christmas "We Wish You a Merry Christmas")", "[The First Noel](/wiki/The_First_Noel "The First Noel")", "[I Saw Three Ships](/wiki/I_Saw_Three_Ships "I Saw Three Ships")" and "[God Rest Ye Merry, Gentlemen](/wiki/God_Rest_Ye_Merry,_Gentlemen "God Rest Ye Merry, Gentlemen")".

Early English composers in classical music include Renaissance artists [Thomas Tallis](/wiki/Thomas_Tallis "Thomas Tallis") and [William Byrd](/wiki/William_Byrd "William Byrd"), followed by [Henry Purcell](/wiki/Henry_Purcell "Henry Purcell") from the [Baroque period](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music") and [Thomas Arne](/wiki/Thomas_Arne "Thomas Arne") who was well known for his patriotic song [Rule, Britannia!](/wiki/Rule,_Britannia! "Rule, Britannia!"). German-born [George Frideric Handel](/wiki/George_Frideric_Handel "George Frideric Handel") spent most of his composing life in London and became a national icon in Britain, creating some of the most well-known works of classical music, especially his English oratorios, *[The Messiah](/wiki/Messiah_(Handel) "Messiah (Handel)")*, *[Solomon](/wiki/Solomon_(Handel) "Solomon (Handel)")*, *[Water Music](/wiki/Water_Music_(Handel) "Water Music (Handel)")*, and *[Music for the Royal Fireworks](/wiki/Music_for_the_Royal_Fireworks "Music for the Royal Fireworks")*.[[293]](#cite_note-305)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/The_Beatles_arrive_at_JFK_Airport.jpg/250px-The_Beatles_arrive_at_JFK_Airport.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Beatles_arrive_at_JFK_Airport.jpg)

[The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") are the most commercially successful and critically acclaimed band in popular music.[[294]](#cite_note-306)

Classical music attracted much attention in the 18th century with the formation of the [Birmingham Triennial Music Festival](/wiki/Birmingham_Triennial_Music_Festival "Birmingham Triennial Music Festival"), which was the longest running classical music festival of its kind until the final concerts in 1912. The [English Musical Renaissance](/wiki/English_Musical_Renaissance "English Musical Renaissance") was a hypothetical development in the late 19th and early 20th century, when English composers, often those lecturing or trained at the [Royal College of Music](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Music "Royal College of Music"), were said to have freed themselves from foreign musical influences. There was a revival in the profile of composers from England in the 20th century led by [Edward Elgar](/wiki/Edward_Elgar "Edward Elgar"), [Benjamin Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten"), [Frederick Delius](/wiki/Frederick_Delius "Frederick Delius"), [Gustav Holst](/wiki/Gustav_Holst "Gustav Holst"), [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams") and others.[[295]](#cite_note-307) Present-day composers from England include [Michael Nyman](/wiki/Michael_Nyman "Michael Nyman"), best known for *[The Piano](/wiki/The_Piano "The Piano")*, and [Andrew Lloyd Webber](/wiki/Andrew_Lloyd_Webber "Andrew Lloyd Webber"), whose musicals have achieved enormous success in the [West End](/wiki/West_End_theatre "West End theatre") and worldwide.

In [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music"), many English bands and solo artists have been cited as the most influential and best-selling musicians of all time. Acts such as [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), [Led Zeppelin](/wiki/Led_Zeppelin "Led Zeppelin"), [Pink Floyd](/wiki/Pink_Floyd "Pink Floyd"), [Elton John](/wiki/Elton_John "Elton John"), [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)"), [Rod Stewart](/wiki/Rod_Stewart "Rod Stewart"), [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie"), [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") and [Def Leppard](/wiki/Def_Leppard "Def Leppard") are amongst the highest-selling recording artists in the world.[[296]](#cite_note-308) Many musical genres have origins in (or strong associations with) England, such as [British invasion](/wiki/British_invasion "British invasion"), [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock"), [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock"), [Mod](/wiki/Mod_(subculture) "Mod (subculture)"), [glam rock](/wiki/Glam_rock "Glam rock"), [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music"), [Britpop](/wiki/Britpop "Britpop"), [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock"), [gothic rock](/wiki/Gothic_rock "Gothic rock"), [shoegazing](/wiki/Shoegazing "Shoegazing"), [acid house](/wiki/Acid_house "Acid house"), [garage](/wiki/UK_garage "UK garage"), [trip hop](/wiki/Trip_hop "Trip hop"), [drum and bass](/wiki/Drum_and_bass "Drum and bass") and [dubstep](/wiki/Dubstep "Dubstep").[[297]](#cite_note-309)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Royal_Albert_Hall_Rear%2C_London%2C_England_-_Diliff.jpg/250px-Royal_Albert_Hall_Rear%2C_London%2C_England_-_Diliff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Albert_Hall_Rear,_London,_England_-_Diliff.jpg)

The [Royal Albert Hall](/wiki/Royal_Albert_Hall "Royal Albert Hall"). Since the hall's opening in 1871, the world's leading artists from many performance genres have appeared on its stage.

Large outdoor [music festivals](/wiki/List_of_music_festivals_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of music festivals in the United Kingdom") in the summer and autumn are popular, such as [Glastonbury](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival"), [V Festival](/wiki/V_Festival "V Festival"), and the [Reading and Leeds Festivals](/wiki/Reading_and_Leeds_Festivals "Reading and Leeds Festivals"). England was at the forefront of the illegal, free [rave](/wiki/Rave "Rave") movement from the late 1980s, which inspired the pan-European culture of [teknivals](/wiki/Teknival "Teknival").[[298]](#cite_note-310) The [Boishakhi Mela](/wiki/Boishakhi_Mela "Boishakhi Mela") is a [Bengali New Year](/wiki/Bengali_New_Year "Bengali New Year") festival celebrated by the [British Bangladeshi](/wiki/British_Bangladeshi "British Bangladeshi") community. It is the largest open-air Asian festival in Europe. After the [Notting Hill Carnival](/wiki/Notting_Hill_Carnival "Notting Hill Carnival"), it is the second-largest street festival in the UK, attracting over 80,000 visitors.

The most prominent [opera house](/wiki/Opera_house "Opera house") in England is the [Royal Opera House](/wiki/Royal_Opera_House "Royal Opera House") at [Covent Garden](/wiki/Covent_Garden "Covent Garden").[[299]](#cite_note-foreman-311) [The Proms](/wiki/The_Proms "The Proms") is a major annual cultural event in the English calendar.[[299]](#cite_note-foreman-311) [The Royal Ballet](/wiki/The_Royal_Ballet "The Royal Ballet") is one of the world's foremost classical ballet companies. The [Royal Academy of Music](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Music "Royal Academy of Music") is the oldest [conservatoire](/wiki/Music_school "Music school") in the UK, founded in 1822, receiving its [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") in 1830.[[300]](#cite_note-312) England is home to numerous major orchestras such as the [BBC Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/BBC_Symphony_Orchestra "BBC Symphony Orchestra"), the [Royal Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Royal_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Royal Philharmonic Orchestra"), the [Philharmonia Orchestra](/wiki/Philharmonia_Orchestra "Philharmonia Orchestra"), and the [London Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/London_Symphony_Orchestra "London Symphony Orchestra").[[301]](#cite_note-313) Other forms of entertainment that originated in England include the [circus](/wiki/Circus "Circus")[[302]](#cite_note-314)[[303]](#cite_note-315)[[304]](#cite_note-316) and the [pantomime](/wiki/Pantomime "Pantomime").[[305]](#cite_note-Panto-317)

### Cinema

See also: [Cinema of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cinema of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Peter_O%27Toole_in_Lawrence_of_Arabia.png/250px-Peter_O%27Toole_in_Lawrence_of_Arabia.png)](/wiki/File:Peter_O%27Toole_in_Lawrence_of_Arabia.png)

[Peter O'Toole](/wiki/Peter_O%27Toole "Peter O'Toole") as T. E. Lawrence in [David Lean](/wiki/David_Lean "David Lean")'s 1962 epic *[Lawrence of Arabia](/wiki/Lawrence_of_Arabia_(film) "Lawrence of Arabia (film)")*

England has had a considerable influence on the history of the cinema, producing some of the greatest actors, directors and motion pictures of all time, including [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock"), [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin"), [David Lean](/wiki/David_Lean "David Lean"), [Laurence Olivier](/wiki/Laurence_Olivier "Laurence Olivier"), [Vivien Leigh](/wiki/Vivien_Leigh "Vivien Leigh"), [John Gielgud](/wiki/John_Gielgud "John Gielgud"), [Peter Sellers](/wiki/Peter_Sellers "Peter Sellers"), [Julie Andrews](/wiki/Julie_Andrews "Julie Andrews"), [Michael Caine](/wiki/Michael_Caine "Michael Caine"), [Gary Oldman](/wiki/Gary_Oldman "Gary Oldman"), [Helen Mirren](/wiki/Helen_Mirren "Helen Mirren"), [Kate Winslet](/wiki/Kate_Winslet "Kate Winslet") and [Daniel Day-Lewis](/wiki/Daniel_Day-Lewis "Daniel Day-Lewis"). Hitchcock and Lean are amongst the most critically acclaimed filmmakers.[[306]](#cite_note-318) Hitchcock's *[The Lodger: A Story of the London Fog](/wiki/The_Lodger:_A_Story_of_the_London_Fog "The Lodger: A Story of the London Fog")* (1926) helped shape the [thriller](/wiki/Thriller_film "Thriller film") genre in film, while his 1929 *[Blackmail](/wiki/Blackmail_(1929_film) "Blackmail (1929 film)")* is often regarded as the first British [sound](/wiki/Sound_film#Transition:_Europe "Sound film") feature film.[[307]](#cite_note-319)

Major film studios in England include [Pinewood](/wiki/Pinewood_Studios "Pinewood Studios"), [Elstree](/wiki/Elstree_Studios "Elstree Studios") and [Shepperton](/wiki/Shepperton_Studios "Shepperton Studios"). Some of the most commercially successful films of all time have been produced in England, including two of the [highest-grossing film franchises](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_film_series "List of highest-grossing film series") (*[Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter_(film_series) "Harry Potter (film series)")* and *[James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond_(film_series) "James Bond (film series)")*).[[308]](#cite_note-320) [Ealing Studios](/wiki/Ealing_Studios "Ealing Studios") in London has a claim to being the oldest continuously working film studio in the world.[[309]](#cite_note-321) Famous for recording many motion picture [film scores](/wiki/Film_scores "Film scores"), the [London Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/London_Symphony_Orchestra "London Symphony Orchestra") first performed film music in 1935.[[310]](#cite_note-322) The [Hammer Horror](/wiki/Hammer_Film_Productions "Hammer Film Productions") films starring [Christopher Lee](/wiki/Christopher_Lee "Christopher Lee") saw the production of the first gory horror films showing blood and guts in colour.[[311]](#cite_note-323)

The [BFI Top 100 British films](/wiki/BFI_Top_100_British_films "BFI Top 100 British films") includes *[Monty Python's Life of Brian](/wiki/Monty_Python%27s_Life_of_Brian "Monty Python's Life of Brian")* (1979), a film regularly voted the funniest of all time by the UK public.[[312]](#cite_note-324) English producers are also active in [international co-productions](/wiki/International_co-productions "International co-productions") and English actors, directors and crew feature regularly in American films. The UK film council ranked [David Yates](/wiki/David_Yates "David Yates"), [Christopher Nolan](/wiki/Christopher_Nolan "Christopher Nolan"), [Mike Newell](/wiki/Mike_Newell_(director) "Mike Newell (director)"), [Ridley Scott](/wiki/Ridley_Scott "Ridley Scott") and [Paul Greengrass](/wiki/Paul_Greengrass "Paul Greengrass") the five most commercially successful English directors since 2001.[[313]](#cite_note-325) Other contemporary English directors include [Sam Mendes](/wiki/Sam_Mendes "Sam Mendes"), [Guy Ritchie](/wiki/Guy_Ritchie "Guy Ritchie") and [Richard Curtis](/wiki/Richard_Curtis "Richard Curtis"). Current actors include [Tom Hardy](/wiki/Tom_Hardy "Tom Hardy"), [Daniel Craig](/wiki/Daniel_Craig "Daniel Craig"), [Benedict Cumberbatch](/wiki/Benedict_Cumberbatch "Benedict Cumberbatch"), [Lena Headey](/wiki/Lena_Headey "Lena Headey"), [Felicity Jones](/wiki/Felicity_Jones "Felicity Jones"), [Emilia Clarke](/wiki/Emilia_Clarke "Emilia Clarke"), [Lashana Lynch](/wiki/Lashana_Lynch "Lashana Lynch"), and [Emma Watson](/wiki/Emma_Watson "Emma Watson"). Acclaimed for his motion capture work, [Andy Serkis](/wiki/Andy_Serkis "Andy Serkis") opened [The Imaginarium Studios](/wiki/The_Imaginarium_Studios "The Imaginarium Studios") in London in 2011.[[314]](#cite_note-326) The visual effects company [Framestore](/wiki/Framestore "Framestore") in London has produced some of the most critically acclaimed special effects in modern film.[[315]](#cite_note-327) Many successful Hollywood films have been based on English people, [stories](/wiki/British_literature "British literature") or events. The 'English Cycle' of Disney animated films include *[Alice in Wonderland](/wiki/Alice_in_Wonderland_(1951_film) "Alice in Wonderland (1951 film)")*, *[The Jungle Book](/wiki/The_Jungle_Book_(1967_film) "The Jungle Book (1967 film)")* and *[Winnie the Pooh](/wiki/The_Many_Adventures_of_Winnie_the_Pooh "The Many Adventures of Winnie the Pooh")*.[[316]](#cite_note-328)

### Sites and institutions

Further information: [List of museums in England](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_England "List of museums in England")

[![A museum building entrance.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/London_Natural_History_Museum_Panorama.jpg/500px-London_Natural_History_Museum_Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:London_Natural_History_Museum_Panorama.jpg)

The [Natural History Museum](/wiki/Natural_History_Museum,_London "Natural History Museum, London") in London

[English Heritage](/wiki/English_Heritage "English Heritage") is a governmental body with a broad remit of managing the historic sites, artefacts and environments of England. It is currently sponsored by the [Department for Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Department_for_Digital,_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport"). A non-governmental charity, the [National Trust](/wiki/National_Trust "National Trust") holds a complementary role, focussed on landscapes and [country houses](/wiki/English_country_house "English country house"). 17 of the 25 United Kingdom UNESCO [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") fall within England.[[317]](#cite_note-329) Some of the best-known of these are: [Hadrian's Wall](/wiki/Hadrian%27s_Wall "Hadrian's Wall"), [Stonehenge, Avebury and Associated Sites](/wiki/Stonehenge,_Avebury_and_Associated_Sites "Stonehenge, Avebury and Associated Sites"), the [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London"), the [Jurassic Coast](/wiki/Jurassic_Coast "Jurassic Coast"), [Saltaire](/wiki/Saltaire "Saltaire"), [Ironbridge Gorge](/wiki/Ironbridge_Gorge "Ironbridge Gorge"), [Blenheim Palace](/wiki/Blenheim_Palace "Blenheim Palace") and the [Lake District](/wiki/Lake_District "Lake District").[[318]](#cite_note-330)

London's [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum") holds more than seven million objects,[[319]](#cite_note-331) one of the largest and most comprehensive collections in the world,[[320]](#cite_note-332) illustrating and documenting global human culture from its beginnings to the present. The [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library") in London is the [national library](/wiki/National_library "National library") and is one of the world's largest [research libraries](/wiki/Research_libraries "Research libraries"), holding over 150 million items in almost all known languages and formats, including around 25 million books.[[321]](#cite_note-333)[[322]](#cite_note-334) The [National Gallery](/wiki/National_Gallery "National Gallery") in [Trafalgar Square](/wiki/Trafalgar_Square "Trafalgar Square") houses a collection of over 2,300 paintings dating from the mid-13th century to 1900.[[323]](#cite_note-335) The [Tate](/wiki/Tate "Tate") galleries house the national collections of British and international modern art; they also host the [Turner Prize](/wiki/Turner_Prize "Turner Prize").[[324]](#cite_note-336)

The [Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Digital,_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Secretary of State for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport") has overall responsibility for cultural property and heritage.[[325]](#cite_note-:0-337)[[326]](#cite_note-338) A [blue plaque](/wiki/Blue_plaque "Blue plaque"), the oldest [historical marker](/wiki/Historical_marker "Historical marker") scheme in the world, is a permanent sign installed in a public place in England to commemorate a link between that location and a famous person or event. In 2011 there were around 1,600 museums in England.[[327]](#cite_note-339) Entry to most museums and galleries is free.[[328]](#cite_note-340) [London](/wiki/London "London") is one of the world's most visited cities, regularly taking the top five most visited cities in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"). It is considered a global centre of finance, art and culture.[[329]](#cite_note-341)

### Media

Main article: [Media in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Media_in_the_United_Kingdom "Media in the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Footbridge_to_Media_City%2C_Salford_-_geograph.org.uk_-_6131368.jpg/250px-Footbridge_to_Media_City%2C_Salford_-_geograph.org.uk_-_6131368.jpg)](/wiki/File:Footbridge_to_Media_City,_Salford_-_geograph.org.uk_-_6131368.jpg)

[MediaCity](/wiki/MediaCityUK "MediaCityUK") in Manchester is the largest media-production facility in Europe.[[330]](#cite_note-342)

The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), founded in 1922, is the UK's publicly funded radio, television and Internet broadcasting corporation, and is the oldest and largest broadcaster in the world.[[331]](#cite_note-MediaNewsline-343)[[332]](#cite_note-AboutBBC-344) It operates numerous television and radio stations in the UK and abroad and its domestic services are funded by the [television licence](/wiki/Television_licensing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television licensing in the United Kingdom").[[333]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-345)[[334]](#cite_note-346) The [BBC World Service](/wiki/BBC_World_Service "BBC World Service") is an [international broadcaster](/wiki/International_broadcasting "International broadcasting") owned and operated by the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"). It is the world's largest of any kind.[[335]](#cite_note-347) It broadcasts radio news, speech and discussions in more than 40 languages.[[336]](#cite_note-348)[[337]](#cite_note-349)

London dominates the media sector in England: national newspapers and television and radio are largely based there, although [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester") is also a significant national media centre. The UK publishing sector, including books, directories and databases, journals, magazines and business media, newspapers and news agencies, has a combined turnover of around £20 billion and employs around 167,000 people.[[338]](#cite_note-350) National newspapers produced in England include *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")*, *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*, and the *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")*.[[339]](#cite_note-paper2-351)

Magazines and journals published in England that have achieved worldwide circulation include *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")*, *[New Scientist](/wiki/New_Scientist "New Scientist")*, *[The Spectator](/wiki/The_Spectator "The Spectator")*, *[Prospect](/wiki/Prospect_(magazine) "Prospect (magazine)")*, *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* and *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")*. The [Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Digital,_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Secretary of State for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport") has overall responsibility over media and broadcasting in England.[[325]](#cite_note-:0-337)

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in England](/wiki/Sport_in_England "Sport in England")

England has a strong sporting heritage, and during the 19th century [codified](/wiki/Codification_(law) "Codification (law)") many sports that are now played around the world. Sports originating in England include [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"),[[340]](#cite_note-football-352) [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"), [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"), [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis"), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), badminton, [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)"),[[341]](#cite_note-353) [rounders](/wiki/Rounders "Rounders"),[[342]](#cite_note-354) [hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey"), [snooker](/wiki/Snooker "Snooker"), [billiards](/wiki/Billiards "Billiards"), [darts](/wiki/Darts "Darts"), table tennis, [bowls](/wiki/Bowls "Bowls"), [netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball"), [thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") horseracing, [greyhound racing](/wiki/Greyhound_racing "Greyhound racing") and [fox hunting](/wiki/Fox_hunting "Fox hunting"). It has helped the development of [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"), sailing and [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One"). England has been crowned world champion in several major sports including: [Cricket](/wiki/2019_Cricket_World_Cup_final "2019 Cricket World Cup final"), [Rugby](/wiki/2003_Rugby_World_Cup_final "2003 Rugby World Cup final") and [Association Football](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup_final "1966 FIFA World Cup final").

Football is the [most popular](/wiki/Sport_in_the_United_Kingdom#Popularity "Sport in the United Kingdom") of these sports. The [England national football team](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team"), whose home venue is [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium"), played [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") in the first-ever international football match in 1872.[[343]](#cite_note-355) Referred to as the "home of football" by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA"), England hosted and won the [1966 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup").[[344]](#cite_note-356) With a British television audience peak of 32.30 million viewers, the [final](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup_Final "1966 FIFA World Cup Final") is the [most watched television event ever](/wiki/List_of_most-watched_television_broadcasts#United_Kingdom "List of most-watched television broadcasts") in the UK.[[345]](#cite_note-357) England is recognised by FIFA as the birthplace of club football: [Sheffield F.C.](/wiki/Sheffield_F.C. "Sheffield F.C."), founded in 1857, is the world's oldest club.[[340]](#cite_note-football-352) The [England women's national football team](/wiki/England_women%27s_national_football_team "England women's national football team") won the [UEFA Women's Euro 2022](/wiki/UEFA_Women%27s_Euro_2022 "UEFA Women's Euro 2022"), hosted by England.[[346]](#cite_note-358) England was also victorious in the [UEFA Women's Euro 2025](/wiki/UEFA_Women%27s_Euro_2025 "UEFA Women's Euro 2025"), winning the final in a penalty shootout against Spain.[[347]](#cite_note-359)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Euro_2022_Final_England_v_Germany_%2852254519898%29.jpg/250px-Euro_2022_Final_England_v_Germany_%2852254519898%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Euro_2022_Final_England_v_Germany_(52254519898).jpg)

[Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium"), home of the [England football team](/wiki/Football_in_England "Football in England"), during the [UEFA Women's Euro 2022 final](/wiki/UEFA_Women%27s_Euro_2022_final "UEFA Women's Euro 2022 final"). At 90,000 capacity, it is the biggest stadium in the UK and [the second-largest stadium in Europe](/wiki/List_of_European_stadiums_by_capacity "List of European stadiums by capacity").

[Cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") is generally thought to have been developed in the early medieval period amongst the farming and metalworking communities of the [Weald](/wiki/Weald "Weald").[[348]](#cite_note-360) The [England cricket team](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team") is a composite England and Wales team. One of the game's top rivalries is [The Ashes](/wiki/The_Ashes "The Ashes") series between England and [Australia](/wiki/Australia_cricket_team "Australia cricket team"), contested since 1882. [Lord's Cricket Ground](/wiki/Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground "Lord's Cricket Ground") situated in London is sometimes referred to as the "Mecca of Cricket".[[349]](#cite_note-361) After winning the [2019 Cricket World Cup](/wiki/2019_Cricket_World_Cup "2019 Cricket World Cup"), England became the first country to win the World Cups in football, rugby union, and cricket.[[350]](#cite_note-362)

[William Penny Brookes](/wiki/William_Penny_Brookes "William Penny Brookes") was prominent in organising the format for the modern [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games").[[351]](#cite_note-363) London has hosted the [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") three times, in [1908](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics"), [1948](/wiki/1948_Summer_Olympics "1948 Summer Olympics"), and [2012](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"). England competes in the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), held every four years. [Sport England](/wiki/Sport_England "Sport England") is the governing body responsible for distributing funds and providing strategic guidance for sporting activity in England.

[Rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") originated in [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School"), Warwickshire in the early 19th century.[[352]](#cite_note-364) The top level of club participation is the [English Premiership](/wiki/Guinness_Premiership "Guinness Premiership"). England's [Red Roses](/wiki/England_women%27s_national_rugby_union_team "England women's national rugby union team") won the [2025 Women's Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2025_Women%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "2025 Women's Rugby World Cup").[[353]](#cite_note-365) [Rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") was born in [Huddersfield](/wiki/Huddersfield "Huddersfield") in 1895. Since 2008, the [England national rugby league team](/wiki/England_national_rugby_league_team "England national rugby league team") has been a full test nation in lieu of the [Great Britain national rugby league team](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team"), which won three [World Cups](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup"). Club sides play in [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League"), the present-day embodiment of the [Rugby Football League Championship](/wiki/Rugby_Football_League_Championship "Rugby Football League Championship"). Rugby League is most popular amongst towns in the northern English counties of [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire"), Yorkshire and [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria").[[354]](#cite_note-366)

[Golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf") has been prominent in England, due in part to its [cultural and geographical ties to Scotland](/wiki/Golf_in_Scotland "Golf in Scotland").[[355]](#cite_note-367) There are professional tours for men and women, in two main tours: the [PGA](/wiki/Professional_Golfers%27_Association_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "Professional Golfers' Association (Great Britain and Ireland)") and the [European Tour](/wiki/European_Tour "European Tour"). The world's oldest golf tournament, and golf's first major is [The Open Championship](/wiki/The_Open_Championship "The Open Championship"), played both in England and Scotland. The biennial golf competition, the [Ryder Cup](/wiki/Ryder_Cup "Ryder Cup"), is named after English businessman [Samuel Ryder](/wiki/Samuel_Ryder "Samuel Ryder").[[356]](#cite_note-368)

[Tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis") was created in Birmingham in the late 19th century, and [the Wimbledon Championships](/wiki/The_Championships,_Wimbledon "The Championships, Wimbledon") is the oldest tennis tournament in the world, and widely considered the most prestigious.[[357]](#cite_note-369) Wimbledon has a major place in the English cultural calendar.[[358]](#cite_note-370)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Mansell_in_his_Ferrari_641_-_1990_British_GP.jpg/250px-Mansell_in_his_Ferrari_641_-_1990_British_GP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mansell_in_his_Ferrari_641_-_1990_British_GP.jpg)

Former [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") world champion [Nigel Mansell](/wiki/Nigel_Mansell "Nigel Mansell") driving at [Silverstone](/wiki/Silverstone_Circuit "Silverstone Circuit") in 1990. The circuit hosted the [first ever World Championship Formula One race](/wiki/1950_British_Grand_Prix "1950 British Grand Prix") in 1950.

In [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), under the [Marquess of Queensberry Rules](/wiki/Marquess_of_Queensberry_Rules "Marquess of Queensberry Rules"), England has produced many world champions across the weight divisions internationally recognised by the governing bodies.[[359]](#cite_note-371)

Originating in 17th and 18th-century England, the [thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") is a horse breed best known for its use in [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing"). The [National Hunt](/wiki/National_Hunt "National Hunt") horse race the [Grand National](/wiki/Grand_National "Grand National"), is held annually at [Aintree Racecourse](/wiki/Aintree_Racecourse "Aintree Racecourse") in early April. It is the most watched horse race in the UK, and three-time winner [Red Rum](/wiki/Red_Rum "Red Rum") is the most successful racehorse in the event's history.[[360]](#cite_note-372)

The [1950 British Grand Prix](/wiki/1950_British_Grand_Prix "1950 British Grand Prix") at [Silverstone](/wiki/Silverstone_Circuit "Silverstone Circuit") was the first race in the newly created [Formula One World Championship](/wiki/Formula_One_World_Championship "Formula One World Championship").[[361]](#cite_note-373)[[362]](#cite_note-374) England has seen the manufacture some of the most technically advanced racing cars, and many of today's racing companies choose England as their base of operations.[[363]](#cite_note-375) England also has a rich heritage in [Grand Prix motorcycle racing](/wiki/Grand_Prix_motorcycle_racing "Grand Prix motorcycle racing"), the premier championship of motorcycle [road racing](/wiki/Road_racing "Road racing"), and has produced several world champions.[[364]](#cite_note-376)

[Darts](/wiki/Darts "Darts") is a widely popular sport in England; a professional competitive sport, it is a traditional [pub game](/wiki/Pub_game "Pub game").[[365]](#cite_note-377)[[366]](#cite_note-378) Another popular sport commonly associated with pub games is [snooker](/wiki/Snooker "Snooker"), and England has produced several world champions.

The English are keen sailors and enjoy competitive [sailing](/wiki/Sailing "Sailing"); founding and winning some of the world's most famous international competitive tournaments across the various race formats, including the [match race](/wiki/Match_race "Match race"), a regatta, and the [America's Cup](/wiki/America%27s_Cup "America's Cup").

## National symbols

Main article: [National symbols of England](/wiki/National_symbols_of_England "National symbols of England")

[![A red and white flower.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Tudor_Rose.svg/250px-Tudor_Rose.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tudor_Rose.svg)

The [Tudor rose](/wiki/Tudor_rose "Tudor rose"), England's [national floral emblem](/wiki/National_flower "National flower")

[![A red shield tapers to its bottom end; on it are three stylised golden lions with blue claws.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Royal_arms_of_England.svg/250px-Royal_arms_of_England.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Royal_arms_of_England.svg)

The [Royal Arms of England](/wiki/Royal_Arms_of_England "Royal Arms of England")

The St George's Cross has been the national [flag of England](/wiki/Flag_of_England "Flag of England") since the 13th century. Originally, the flag was used by the maritime [Republic of Genoa](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa"). The English monarch paid a tribute to the [Doge of Genoa](/wiki/Doge_of_Genoa "Doge of Genoa") from 1190 onwards so that English ships could fly the flag as a means of protection when entering the Mediterranean.
A red cross was a symbol for many [Crusaders](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades") in the 12th and 13th centuries, and became associated with [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George").[[367]](#cite_note-379) Since 1606 the St George's Cross has formed part of the design of the [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag"), a Pan-British flag designed by King [James I](/wiki/James_I_of_England "James I of England").[[227]](#cite_note-flaghistory-234) During the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") and [Interregnum](/wiki/Interregnum_(England) "Interregnum (England)"), the [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army")'s standards and the [Commonwealth](/wiki/English_Commonwealth "English Commonwealth")'s [Great Seal](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_the_Realm#Commonwealth "Great Seal of the Realm") both incorporated the flag of Saint George.[[368]](#cite_note-380)[[369]](#cite_note-381)

There are numerous other symbols and symbolic artefacts, both official and unofficial, including the [Tudor rose](/wiki/Tudor_rose "Tudor rose"), the nation's [floral emblem](/wiki/National_emblem "National emblem"), and the Three Lions featured on the [Royal Arms of England](/wiki/Royal_Arms_of_England "Royal Arms of England"). The Tudor rose was adopted as a national emblem of England around the time of the [Wars of the Roses](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Roses "Wars of the Roses") as a symbol of peace.[[370]](#cite_note-Flowers-382) It is a [syncretic](/wiki/Syncreticism "Syncreticism") symbol in that it merged the white rose of the [Yorkists](/wiki/House_of_York "House of York") and the red rose of the [Lancastrians](/wiki/House_of_Lancaster "House of Lancaster"). It is also known as the *Rose of England*.[[371]](#cite_note-Rose-383) The [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") tree is a symbol of England: the [Royal Oak](/wiki/Royal_Oak "Royal Oak") symbol and [Oak Apple Day](/wiki/Oak_Apple_Day "Oak Apple Day") commemorate the escape of King [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") after his father's execution, when he hid in an oak to avoid detection by the parliamentarians before safely reaching exile.

The Royal Arms of England, a national [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") featuring three lions, originated with [Richard the Lionheart](/wiki/Richard_the_Lionheart "Richard the Lionheart") in 1198. It is [blazoned](/wiki/Blazon "Blazon") as *gules, three lions passant guardant or* and it provides one of the most prominent symbols of England. England does not have an official national anthem, as the United Kingdom as a whole has *[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")*. However, *[Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem_(hymn) "Jerusalem (hymn)")*, *[Land of Hope and Glory](/wiki/Land_of_Hope_and_Glory "Land of Hope and Glory")* (used for England during the [2002 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games")),[[372]](#cite_note-384) and *[I Vow to Thee, My Country](/wiki/I_Vow_to_Thee,_My_Country "I Vow to Thee, My Country")* are often considered unofficial [English national anthems](/wiki/National_anthem_of_England "National anthem of England"). England's [National Day](/wiki/National_Day "National Day") is 23 April which is [Saint George's Day](/wiki/St_George%27s_Day_in_England "St George's Day in England"): Saint George is the patron saint of England.[[373]](#cite_note-St._George-385)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** England has no official anthem, as such it typically uses the anthem of the United Kingdom, [God Save The King](/wiki/God_Save_The_King "God Save The King") at international events, although it sometimes makes use of other English patriotic songs such as [Land of Hope and Glory](/wiki/Land_of_Hope_and_Glory "Land of Hope and Glory") and the hymn [Jerusalem](/wiki/And_did_those_feet_in_ancient_time "And did those feet in ancient time").
2. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** Englishman/Englishwoman
3. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** ONS Standard Area Measurement, 'area to mean high water excluding inland water'
4. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** [ONS](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") Standard Area Measurement, 'total extent of the realm' (area to mean low water)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-57)** As [Roger Scruton](/wiki/Roger_Scruton "Roger Scruton") explains, "The Reformation must not be confused with the changes introduced into the Church of England during the "Reformation Parliament" of 1529–36, which were of a political rather than a religious nature, designed to unite the secular and religious sources of authority within a single sovereign power: the Anglican Church did not make substantial change in doctrine until later."[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScruton1982470-56)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-83)** Figure of 550,000 military deaths is for England and Wales.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECox1970180-82)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-204)** For instance, in 1980 around 50 million [Americans](/wiki/United_States "United States") claimed [English ancestry](/wiki/English_Americans "English Americans").[[194]](#cite_note-200) In Canada there are around 6.5 million [Canadians](/wiki/Demographics_of_Canada "Demographics of Canada") who claim [English ancestry](/wiki/English_Canadians "English Canadians").[[195]](#cite_note-201) Around 70% of [Australians](/wiki/Demographics_of_Australia "Demographics of Australia") in 1999 denoted their origins as [Anglo-Celtic](/wiki/Anglo-Celtic "Anglo-Celtic"), a category which includes all peoples from Great Britain and Ireland.[[196]](#cite_note-202) Chileans of [English descent](/wiki/English_Chilean "English Chilean") are somewhat of an anomaly in that [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") itself was never part of the British Empire, but today there are around 420,000 people of English origins living there.[[197]](#cite_note-203)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-pagan_238-0)** People who strictly identified as "Pagan". Other Pagan paths, such as Wicca or Druidism, have not been included in this number.[[230]](#cite_note-2011_ONS_results-237)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-239)** People who strictly identified as "Wiccan". Other Pagan paths, such as Druidism, and general "Pagan" have not been included in this number.[[230]](#cite_note-2011_ONS_results-237)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-250)** Students attending English universities now have to pay tuition fees towards the cost of their education, as do English students who choose to attend university in Scotland. Scottish students attending Scottish universities have their fees paid by the devolved Scottish Parliament.[[240]](#cite_note-House_of_Commons_Library-249)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-264)** While people such as [Norman Foster](/wiki/Norman_Foster_(architect) "Norman Foster (architect)") and [Richard Rogers](/wiki/Richard_Rogers "Richard Rogers") represent the modernist movement, [Prince Charles](/wiki/Prince_Charles "Prince Charles") since the 1980s has voiced strong views against it in favour of traditional architecture and put his ideas into practice at his [Poundbury](/wiki/Poundbury "Poundbury") development in Dorset.[[253]](#cite_note-ArchCon4-263) Architects like [Raymond Erith](/wiki/Raymond_Erith "Raymond Erith"), [Francis Johnson](/wiki/Francis_Johnson_(architect) "Francis Johnson (architect)") and [Quinlan Terry](/wiki/Quinlan_Terry "Quinlan Terry") continued to practise in the classical style.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-276)** These tales may have come to prominence, at least in part, as an attempt by the Norman ruling elite to legitimise their rule of the British Isles, finding [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon") history ill-suited to the task during an era when members of the deposed [House of Wessex](/wiki/House_of_Wessex "House of Wessex"), especially [Edgar the Ætheling](/wiki/Edgar_the_%C3%86theling "Edgar the Ætheling") and his nephews of the Scottish [House of Dunkeld](/wiki/House_of_Dunkeld "House of Dunkeld"), were still active in the isles.[[262]](#cite_note-higham1-273)[[264]](#cite_note-275) Also [Michael Wood](/wiki/Michael_Wood_(historian) "Michael Wood (historian)") explains; "Over the centuries the figure of Arthur became a symbol of British history – a way of explaining the matter of Britain, the relationship between the Saxons and the Celts, and a way of exorcising ghosts and healing the wounds of the past."[[261]](#cite_note-woodbbc-272)
